articles.title: Ireland’s Black community opens up about racism after ‘George Floyd moment’
articles.summary: In the wake of Yves Sakila's death following a violent restraint, many say racism in Ireland is an overlooked scourge.
articles.titleZh: “乔治·弗洛伊德时刻”后，爱尔兰黑人社区公开谈论种族主义
articles.summaryZh: 伊夫·萨基拉（Yves Sakila）因暴力束缚而死亡后，许多人表示爱尔兰的种族主义是一个被忽视的祸害。

contentParagraphs.en: Last month, 40-year-old Emer O’Neill, a Black Irish woman, was racially insulted three times.Teenagers in her town south of Dublin shouted, “Go back to your country!” at her, she was rudely asked by a man whether she spoke English, and she was called the n-word at a local pub – all in the space of two weeks.Recommended Stories list of 1 itemlist 1 of 1Hundreds protest Ireland’s ‘George Floyd moment’end of list“I don’t have another country to go to. This is my country,” said O’Neill, an activist and broadcaster who in recent years has presented Dublin’s St Patrick’s Day parade for Ireland’s national television channel, RTE.Days later, she found herself shaking with emotion while singing at an event to remember Yves Sakila, a 35-year-old who was killed on May 15 outside Arnotts, a department store in central Dublin. In video footage by bystanders, the shop’s security guards who restrained him appear to have placed their knees on his neck for more than four minutes.Sakila, an Irish national, immigrated from the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) when he was 13. The death has been dubbed Ireland’s George Floyd moment, reminiscent of the 2020 killing of the 46-year-old Black man in the US state of Minnesota at the hands of white police that set off mass antiracism protests.Sakila was allegedly suspected of shoplifting and is said to have accidentally knocked down a man when rushing out of the department store. Police arrived and handcuffed him. They performed cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) when they saw he was unwell, but he was later pronounced dead at Dublin’s Mater Hospital.As a teenager, he struggled when his adoptive parents separated, and at the time of his death, he had been living on the streets.“He landed in care services at 16, but he never got back to normal. Even though his adopted mother wanted to bring him home, he wanted freedom,” said Lassane Ouedraogo of Africa Solidarity Centre, who first met him five years ago. Like other homeless people, Sakila was being supported by the diaspora-led organisation.Ouedraogo described him as a “gentleman” with whom he had “lovely conversations”. “He needed help, not a death sentence.”No arrests have been made over his death.“We don’t need specialists to see the video and understand how he died,” Ouedraogo said.Top row from left: Jackie McCarthy O’Brien, former Irish international football and rugby player; Lassane Ouedraogo of the Africa Solidarity Centre and literary critic Sandrine Ndahiro. Bottom row from left: Emer O’Neill, a broadcaster, author and activist; Zainab Obasuyi, a researcher at Technological University Dublin and social psychologist Mamobo Ogoro [Al Jazeera]In the wake of the incident, members of Ireland’s minority communities have described a sense of denial about racism in a country known for an anticolonial spirit.Days before Sakila died, Bertie Ahern, Ireland’s former taoiseach (prime minister), was filmed saying, “We can’t be taking in people from the Congo and all these places,” while canvassing for his centre-right Fianna Fail party for a local by-election. Incumbent Taoiseach Micheal Martin – also from Fianna Fail – said while he did not approve of Ahern’s comments, his party cannot stop people from canvassing.Sandrine Ndahiro, a literary critic of Black and postcolonial literature and culture at Maynooth University, said she cried for the duration of a prayer vigil outside the Arnotts store last month.“The shop stayed open for business. People were going in and out, as if nothing had happened. They would have shut if a white person had died,” she said.‘Too scared to be called racist’Zainab Obasuyi, a PhD researcher at Technological University Dublin, said she has also experienced racism. In high school, her classmates chanted “Ebola la la” upon seeing her.“Every time I speak about racism, I’m told, ‘You are too sensitive, you are overreacting, you are misinterpreting.’ Irish society is too scared to be called racist because it’s viewed as a moral failing, and hence they throw these words as a defence,” said Obasuyi, now 24. She is part of Black and Irish, a nonprofit advocacy group coordinating a coalition to memorialise Sakila.For Jackie McCarthy O’Brien, who represented Ireland in international football and rugby in the 1980s and 90s, becoming the first Black woman in Ireland to play both sports, the fields felt freer.“The only way people wouldn’t question my Irish identity was if I wore the green jersey,” she said. “I was a giant on the pitch. Off the pitch, I was the Black kid with the giant head. The 90 minutes of the game was pure freedom. But when you speak up, you are deemed the angry Black woman and an aggressor who rocks the boat.”Although O’Brien is well-known across Ireland, the comments she faces are still upsetting. “People have told me, ‘You are not really Black,’ or ‘I don’t see colour.’ But why can’t they see my colour when I see their white skin?”O’Neill said unconscious bias and stereotyping are difficult to digest because they contradict what Ireland is known for, such as its solidarity with Palestine and South Africa in the past.“Smaller Irish towns have banners everywhere saying Ireland is only for the Irish. The racism is no longer subtle,” said Ndahiro, the literary critic.In some Irish news outlets, Sakila, a naturalised citizen, has been referred to as a “Congolese man”.“A Black migrant is expected to demonstrate excellence and win medals to be deemed Irish. Sakila’s Irishness got stripped away immediately,” Ndahiro said. “How can you write about feminism, human rights and racism but not show up for protests? Irish people whose timelines are all about Palestine online have not uttered a single word about Sakila’s death.”At a recent antiracism demonstration outside Leinster House, the Irish parliament, a smaller group of counter-protesters called on “foreigners” to leave Ireland.A Central Statistics Office survey in 2025 found that 49 percent of “Black Irish, Black African and other
contentParagraphs.zh: 上个月，40岁的爱尔兰黑人女性埃默·奥尼尔三度受到种族侮辱。她所在的都柏林南部小镇的青少年高喊：“滚回你的国家去！”一名男子粗鲁地问她是否会说英语，她在当地一家酒吧被称为“黑鬼”——这一切都发生在两周之内。 推荐故事列表 1 条列表 1 百条抗议爱尔兰的“乔治·弗洛伊德时刻”列表末尾 “我没有其他国家可去。这是我的国家，”奥尼尔说，他是一位活动家和广播员，近年来曾介绍过都柏林的圣帕特里克节爱尔兰国家电视频道 RTE 的日间游行。几天后，她在一场纪念伊夫·萨基拉 (Yves Sakila) 的活动中唱歌时发现自己激动得发抖。伊夫·萨基拉是一位 35 岁的男子，于 5 月 15 日在都柏林市中心阿诺茨百货公司外被杀。在旁观者的视频片段中，束缚他的商店保安似乎将膝盖放在他的脖子上超过四分钟。萨基拉是爱尔兰人，13岁时从刚果民主共和国移民。这起死亡事件被称为爱尔兰的乔治·弗洛伊德时刻，让人想起2020年美国明尼苏达州一名46岁黑人被杀害的事件。白人警察引发大规模反种族主义抗议。萨基拉涉嫌入店行窃，据说在冲出百货商店时不小心撞倒了一名男子。警察赶到并给他戴上手铐。当他们发现他身体不适时，他们对他进行了心肺复苏术（CPR），但后来他在都柏林马特医院被宣布死亡。十几岁的时候，他的养父母分居，他一直在挣扎，在他去世时，他一直流落街头。非洲的拉萨内·韦德拉奥戈（Lassane Ouedraogo）说：“他16岁时进入护理服务机构，但他从未恢复正常。尽管他的养母想带他回家，但他想要自由。”团结中心，五年前第一次见到他。和其他无家可归者一样，萨基拉得到了这个侨民领导的组织的支持。韦德拉奥戈形容他是一位“绅士”，与他进行了“愉快的交谈”。 “他需要帮助，而不是死刑。” 尚未因他的死亡而被捕。韦德拉奥戈说：“我们不需要专家观看视频并了解他是如何死亡的。”上排左起：杰基·麦卡锡·奥布莱恩，前爱尔兰国际足球和橄榄球运动员；非洲团结中心的拉萨内·韦德拉奥果 (Lassane Ouedraogo) 和文学评论家桑德琳·恩达希罗 (Sandrine Ndahiro)。下排左起：Emer O’Neill，广播员、作家和活动家；都柏林理工大学研究员扎伊纳布·奥巴苏伊 (Zainab Obasuyi) 和社会心理学家马莫博·奥戈罗 (Mamobo Ogoro) [半岛电视台]事件发生后，爱尔兰少数族裔社区的成员描述了对这个以反殖民精神闻名的国家的种族主义的否认感。 在萨基拉去世前几天，爱尔兰前总理伯蒂·埃亨 (Bertie Ahern) 在视频中说道：“我们不能接收来自爱尔兰的人。”刚果和所有这些地方”，同时为他的中右翼 Fianna Fail 政党进行当地补选游说。现任总理迈克尔·马丁（同样来自 Fianna Fail）表示，虽然他不同意埃亨的言论，但他的政党无法阻止人们拉票。梅努斯大学黑人和后殖民文学与文化的文学评论家桑德琳·恩达希罗 (Sandrine Ndahiro) 表示，上个月她在阿诺特商店外的守夜祈祷期间哭了。都柏林科技大学博士研究员扎伊纳布·奥巴苏伊表示，她也经历过种族主义。高中时，她的同学一看到她就高喊“埃博拉啦啦”。“每次我谈到种族主义时，我都会被告知，‘你太敏感了，你反应过度，你误解了。’爱尔兰社会太害怕被称为种族主义者，因为这被视为道德败坏，因此他们用这些话作为辩护，”现年 24 岁的奥巴苏伊说。她是非盈利倡导组织“黑人与爱尔兰”的成员协调一个联盟来纪念萨基拉。杰基·麦卡锡·奥布莱恩 (Jackie McCarthy O’Brien) 在 20 世纪 80 年代和 90 年代代表爱尔兰参加国际足球和橄榄球比赛，成为爱尔兰第一位参加这两项运动的黑人女性，球场上感觉更加自由。“人们不会质疑我的爱尔兰身份的唯一方法就是我穿绿色球衣，”她说。 “在球场上，我是一个巨人。在球场外，我是那个长着巨大脑袋的黑人孩子。90分钟的比赛是纯粹的自由。但当你发声时，你会被视为愤怒的黑人女性和破坏现状的侵略者。”尽管奥布莱恩在爱尔兰闻名，但她所面临的评论仍然令人不安。 “人们告诉我，‘你不是真正的黑人’，或者‘我看不到肤色’。但为什么当我看到他们白皙的皮肤时，他们看不到我的肤色呢？”奥尼尔说，无意识的偏见和刻板印象很难消化，因为它们与爱尔兰的著名之处相矛盾，比如过去与巴勒斯坦和南非的团结。“爱尔兰的小城镇到处都有横幅，说爱尔兰只适合爱尔兰人。种族主义不再是微妙的，”恩达希罗说，在一些爱尔兰新闻媒体中，入籍公民萨基拉被称为“刚果人”。“黑人移民应该表现出卓越并赢得奖牌才能被视为爱尔兰人。萨基拉的爱尔兰血统立即被剥夺了，”恩达希罗说。 “你怎么能写关于女权主义、人权和种族主义的文章而不参加抗议活动呢？网上的时间线都是关于巴勒斯坦的爱尔兰人对萨基拉的死只字未提。”最近在爱尔兰议会伦斯特宫外举行的反种族主义示威中，一小群反抗议者呼吁“外国人”离开爱尔兰。中央统计局 2025 年的一项调查发现，49% 的“爱尔兰黑人、非洲黑人和其他黑人”

articles.summary: The boss of X, Tesla and SpaceX is the world's richest person and has used his platform to make his views known on a vast array of topics.

articles.title: How cellphone carriers prepare for hurricane season with AI, drones and 'cows'
articles.summary: Hurricane season is expected to be milder than usual this year. But that's not stopping cell phone companies from pulling out all the stops.
articles.titleZh: 手机运营商如何利用人工智能、无人机和“奶牛”为飓风季节做好准备
articles.summaryZh: 预计今年的飓风季节将比平常温和。但这并不能阻止手机公司使出浑身解数。

contentParagraphs.en: Residents make phone calls in their neighborhood after heavy rains from hurricane Helene caused record flooding and damage on September 28, 2024 in Asheville, North Carolina.
contentParagraphs.zh: 2024年9月28日，飓风海伦在北卡罗来纳州阿什维尔造成创纪录的洪水和破坏，居民在飓风海伦的暴雨后打电话给邻居。

contentParagraphs.en: Melissa Sue Gerrits/Getty Images North America
contentParagraphs.zh: Melissa Sue Gerrits/Getty Images北美

contentParagraphs.en: Melissa Sue Gerrits/Getty Images North America
contentParagraphs.zh: Melissa Sue Gerrits/Getty Images北美

contentParagraphs.en: Anthony Leone and his wife, Corinne Saunders, have already started watching the weather. In a few weeks, they'll be filling coolers and plugging in generators. He has lived in North Carolina's Outer Banks for more than 20 years. So he's familiar with this routine that precedes the inevitable — hurricane season.
contentParagraphs.zh: Anthony Leone和他的妻子Corinne Saunders已经开始关注天气。几周后，他们将加满冷却器并插入发电机。他在北卡罗来纳州外滩生活了20多年。因此，他熟悉不可避免的飓风季节之前的例行公事。

contentParagraphs.en: As for their cell phones? "We just plan ahead to not use [them]."
contentParagraphs.zh: 他们的手机呢？ “我们只是提前计划好不使用它们。”

contentParagraphs.en: Hurricane season runs from June to November, and the National Weather Service said in May it's predicting below-normal activity in the Atlantic region for the first time since 2015. But the chance for at least one very powerful storm remains.
contentParagraphs.zh: 飓风季节从6月持续到11月，美国国家气象局在5月份表示，自2015年以来首次预测大西洋地区的活动低于正常水平。但至少还有一场非常强大的风暴的机会。

contentParagraphs.en: Even though there may be slightly less hurricane activity this year, high winds and rain can still interrupt the power supply stationary cell towers rely on. As a result, cellphone carriers are fortifying their preparations with newer tools such as artificial intelligence and drones, so customers in the path of a hurricane can continue to communicate with loved ones, work from home and render aid.
contentParagraphs.zh: 尽管今年的飓风活动可能略有减少，但强风和强雨仍然会中断固定基站所依赖的电力供应。因此，手机运营商正在使用人工智能和无人机等新工具加强准备工作，以便处于飓风中的客户可以继续与亲人沟通，在家工作并提供援助。

contentParagraphs.en: Anthony Leone and his wife, Corinne Saunders, kayak in Kitty Hawk Bay, in North Carolina's Outer Banks.
contentParagraphs.zh: Anthony Leone和他的妻子Corinne Saunders在北卡罗来纳州外滩的小鹰湾划皮划艇。

contentParagraphs.en: Anthony Leone/Anthony Leone

contentParagraphs.en: Strategies the companies are using
contentParagraphs.zh: 公司正在使用的策略

contentParagraphs.en: Verizon Wireless starts its hurricane response plans about a week before one is forecasted in an area, according to Srini Kalapala, Verizon's senior vice president of wireless engineering and operations. Before a storm, Verizon uses drones to take pictures of a coverage area. It then takes more pictures after a storm to assess the damage. AI sends a precise location of the damage to engineers and technicians as they start their repairs.
contentParagraphs.zh: 根据Verizon无线工程和运营高级副总裁Srini Kalapala的说法， Verizon Wireless在一个地区预测飓风发生前一周左右开始其飓风应对计划。在暴风雨之前， Verizon使用无人机拍摄覆盖区域的照片。暴风雨过后，它会拍摄更多照片来评估损坏情况。AI会在工程师和技术人员开始维修时向他们发送损坏的精确位置。

contentParagraphs.en: "Hurricanes, especially — you don't understand the damage until it's fully done," Kalapala said.
contentParagraphs.zh: Kalapala说： “特别是飓风，在完全完成之前，您不会了解损失。”

contentParagraphs.en: Verizon has an assortment of mobile tools should the company's stationary cell towers give out during a hurricane. Many of them have animals for codenames; for example, COW stands for "cell on wheels," and a HAWK, or "high altitude wireless kennewhat," is a drone that can carry small towers to provide service from the sky. Kalapala said these tools have helped shave restoration times down to minutes and hours.
contentParagraphs.zh: Verizon has an assortment of mobile tools should the company's stationary cell towers give out during a hurricane. Many of them have animals for codenames; for example, COW stands for "cell on wheels," and a HAWK, or "high altitude wireless kennewhat," is a drone that can carry small towers to provide service from the sky. Kalapala said these tools have helped shave restoration times down to minutes and hours.

contentParagraphs.en: AT&T also makes preparations about a week before a hurricane. Its disaster response fleet includes boats, barges and helicopters that can transport team members and equipment through hard-to-reach areas including swamps and mountains after a disaster, according to Shannon Browning, the associate director of AT&T's Network Disaster Recovery team. The company will often move equipment across the country as needed throughout the year, for events such as hurricanes, snowstorms and wildfires, he said.

contentParagraphs.en: "It used to be [that hurricanes] were very season-specific. And then you started to see that wildfire season crept into hurricane season," he said.
contentParagraphs.zh: "It used to be [that hurricanes] were very season-specific. And then you started to see that wildfire season crept into hurricane season," he said.

contentParagraphs.en: As a result, the company has merged several of its disaster teams into one group, which has helped the company streamline its approach to public safety, Browning said.
contentParagraphs.zh: As a result, the company has merged several of its disaster teams into one group, which has helped the company streamline its approach to public safety, Browning said.

contentParagraphs.en: Jon Freier, T-Mobile's chief operating officer, said the company prepares for disasters three to five months in advance.
contentParagraphs.zh: Jon Freier, T-Mobile's chief operating officer, said the company prepares for disasters three to five months in advance.

contentParagraphs.en: If the power goes out during a storm, it can take a few hours or days to restore service. In the meantime, customers get free satellite service, Freier said, and the company uses AI tools to automatically adjust antennas and conserve backup batteries when needed, which stretches the time period that they can be used.
contentParagraphs.zh: If the power goes out during a storm, it can take a few hours or days to restore service. In the meantime, customers get free satellite service, Freier said, and the company uses AI tools to automatically adjust antennas and conserve backup batteries when needed, which stretches the time period that they can be used.

contentParagraphs.en: "From my experience in these disasters, people will go without power a little bit longer, and they're more patient," Freier said. "But they want that smartphone to work, and to be connected."

contentParagraphs.en: What some users have experienced
contentParagraphs.zh: What some users have experienced

contentParagraphs.en: Leone, the North Carolina resident, has had several cell phone providers over the years, with different levels of performance during hurricanes and other major weather events. With his last carrier, getting regular service was a year-round issue; he could only get a signal on highways and at the beach. He has now been with AT&T for more than a decade, and he said his service has been fairly reliable during hurricanes.
contentParagraphs.zh: Leone, the North Carolina resident, has had several cell phone providers over the years, with different levels of performance during hurricanes and other major weather events. With his last carrier, getting regular service was a year-round issue; he could only get a signal on highways and at the beach. He has now been with AT&T for more than a decade, and he said his service has been fairly reliable during hurricanes.

contentParagraphs.en: "We usually get a text message saying, 'Hey, there's a storm coming,'" and if he loses service in a power outage, "It's only a couple days, usually, before they fix it."
contentParagraphs.zh: "We usually get a text message saying, 'Hey, there's a storm coming,'" and if he loses service in a power outage, "It's only a couple days, usually, before they fix it."

contentParagraphs.en: But before civilians' smartphones power up again, first responders' needs have to be met. Many major companies prioritize and tailor networks specifically for first responders — including law enforcement, firefighters and paramedics.
contentParagraphs.zh: But before civilians' smartphones power up again, first responders' needs have to be met. Many major companies prioritize and tailor networks specifically for first responders — including law enforcement, firefighters and paramedics.

contentParagraphs.en: Peter Antevy is the medical director for several fire departments in Broward County, Fla.. He said his teams rely on carriers' cell and Wi-Fi services not just to make voice calls, but to conduct telemedicine appointments, send test results and transmit live updates from 911 dispatch centers to ambulances.
contentParagraphs.zh: Peter Antevy is the medical director for several fire departments in Broward County, Fla.. He said his teams rely on carriers' cell and Wi-Fi services not just to make voice calls, but to conduct telemedicine appointments, send test results and transmit live updates from 911 dispatch centers to ambulances.

contentParagraphs.en: "There is a lot of data that goes back and forth," he said.
contentParagraphs.zh: "There is a lot of data that goes back and forth," he said.

contentParagraphs.en: So it's important for company representatives to be in the room during local disaster response strategy meetings. Amy Weber, the chief of emergency medical services at the Galveston County Health District in Texas, said representatives from Verizon and AT&T come to her department's meetings to plan what equipment they'll send, and when.
contentParagraphs.zh: So it's important for company representatives to be in the room during local disaster response strategy meetings. Amy Weber, the chief of emergency medical services at the Galveston County Health District in Texas, said representatives from Verizon and AT&T come to her department's meetings to plan what equipment they'll send, and when.

contentParagraphs.en: "Communication is always a huge breakdown for first responders, because we get inundated with calls just from the service area, so putting us at top priority helps us be able to do our job," Weber said.

articles.title: A science powerhouse bets on genetic therapy to beat brain disorders
articles.summary: After spending two decades studying how the brain works, a team of scientists prepares to focus on gene therapy treatments for brain diseases, including Alzheimer's and Parkinson's.
articles.titleZh: 科学巨头押注于基因疗法来战胜大脑疾病
articles.summaryZh: 在花了二十年研究大脑如何工作之后，一组科学家准备专注于治疗脑部疾病的基因疗法，包括阿尔茨海默氏症和帕金森氏症。

contentParagraphs.en: After spending two decades studying how the brain works, a team of scientists prepares to focus on gene therapy treatments for brain diseases, including Alzheimer's and Parkinson's.
contentParagraphs.zh: After spending two decades studying how the brain works, a team of scientists prepares to focus on gene therapy treatments for brain diseases, including Alzheimer's and Parkinson's.

contentParagraphs.en: AILSA CHANG, HOST: Scientists who have spent decades learning how the brain works say they are ready to start fixing it when it breaks. A brain research center in Seattle called the Allen Institute is working to develop genetic therapies for diseases including Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, ALS and Huntington's. Here's NPR's Jon Hamilton.JON HAMILTON, BYLINE: The effort is called the Brain Health accelerator, and it includes scientists like Jeff Carroll. When Carroll was a teenager, he found out his mother had Huntington's disease. It's a fatal inherited disorder that destroys brain cells.JEFF CARROLL: The whole reason I'm in science started with this frustration with not being able to understand what was happening with my mom.HAMILTON: Carroll learned that he, too, carried the Huntington's gene. He spent years at the University of Washington studying mice with the condition, which occurs when the faulty gene tells nerve cells to produce toxic levels of a certain protein. To Carroll, the solution seemed obvious.CARROLL: Since we know all the bad stuff in Huntington's comes from this one gene, let's get rid of the levels of that gene.HAMILTON: But finding a way to do that was beyond the reach of his own lab. So Carroll joined the Allen Institute.CARROLL: It's difficult to do the scale of research that you need with a team of five or six or even 10 people, and so the hundreds of people they have here at the Allen Institute is just an entirely different approach to science.HAMILTON: The scientist in charge of the Brain Health accelerator is Ed Lein. He says it's built on years of basic research.ED LEIN: We now have a complete description of the types of cells that make up the brain and also the genetic underpinnings of their properties. This foundation then lets you study disease.HAMILTON: By seeing how disease changes these cells - Lein says scientists at Allen already have some experience with this approach.LEIN: And in the case of Alzheimer's disease, we can see that it's not only about plaques and tangles, but it affects very specific types of neurons that are lost early in the disease and then over the course of disease.HAMILTON: A genetic therapy designed to protect these neurons might delay or prevent symptoms. Lein says other therapies might preserve the neurons affected by Parkinson's or ALS.LEIN: And we can now turn on a therapeutic gene just in the right kind of cell with these types of technologies, and that opens up the possibility for very specific precision genetic therapies for brain disorders.HAMILTON: One reason that's possible is research funded by the BRAIN Initiative, a program announced by President Obama back in 2013. John Ngai of the National Institutes of Health says it's given scientists new ways to observe the inner workings of the brain.JOHN NGAI: I am shocked at how far we've come in the last 10, 12 years. It's just been beyond my wildest imagination, and I've been accused of having a pretty good imagination.HAMILTON: Ngai says insights from the BRAIN Initiative have already helped researchers find new ways to use existing treatments. Deep brain stimulation, for example, got its start as a way to use electrical pulses to reduce the symptoms of Parkinson's disease.NGAI: Now it's being applied to a whole range of disorders - epilepsy, treatment-resistant depression, OCD, PTSD, binge-eating disorder.HAMILTON: Scientists at the Allen Institute say genetic therapies could follow a similar path. Jeff Carroll, the scientist with the Huntington's gene, says the approach has worked on children with spinal muscular atrophy. It's a genetic condition that destroys motor neurons in the spinal cord.CARROLL: Every kid with this horrible mutation died when they were, like, 18 months for all of human history, and now they're going to high school. So things that were, like, unimaginable can just change.HAMILTON: Carroll says his goal is to help accelerate that change. Jon Hamilton, NPR News.(SOUNDBITE OF THE DARE SONG, "GIRLS")

articles.title: Civilians flee Mogadishu as Somali troops and opposition-allied militias trade fire
articles.summary:
Violence flares before protests on Thursday over president’s decision to remain in office after his term expired
Government troops and militias allied with the opposition have exchanged fire in Somalia’s capital, Mogadishu, damaging property and forcing some civilians to flee.
Th
articles.titleZh: 索马里军队与反对派结盟的民兵交火，平民逃离摩加迪沙
articles.summaryZh:
周四，总统决定在任期届满后继续留任，引发抗议活动，随后爆发暴力事件
政府军和与反对派结盟的民兵在索马里首都摩加迪沙交火，造成财产损失，并迫使一些平民逃离。
钍

contentParagraphs.en: Government troops and militias allied with the opposition have exchanged fire in Somalia’s capital, Mogadishu, damaging property and forcing some civilians to flee.The violence flared up ahead of planned protests on Thursday over president Hassan Sheikh Mohamud’s decision to remain in office after his term expired in May. In March, parliament backed constitutional changes that could allow Mohamud to extend his term by a year and push back an election.The ormer president Sharif Sheikh Ahmed, who was in power from 2009 to 2012, said government forces had targeted his home, and accused Mohamud’s government of “illegally altering the constitution”.“The government forces encircled and attacked my house. I am never scared of their aggressive attack, I will fight back,” he said in a video on his Facebook account posted overnight.In a post on X, the former prime minister Hassan Ali Khaire accused government troops of using heavy weapons including anti-tank weapons and drones in a densely populated area. Khaire added that the government had directed “a sustained and indiscriminate military assault” aimed at killing him and Ahmed.Somalia’s information and defence ministers did not pick up calls or respond to Reuters’ messages seeking comment.Fighting began around 5pm on Wednesday and continued into Thursday morning, with thousands of government troops deployed in Mogadishu’s Howl Wadag and Abdiasis districts, where they traded fire with militias supporting opposition leaders, residents told Reuters.In March, parliament backed constitutional changes that could allow Somalia’s president, Hassan Sheikh Mohamud, pictured, to extend his term by a year. Photograph: Feisal Omar/ReutersOne resident, Ahmed Ismail, told Reuters early on Thursday: “A mortar shell landed on my neighbour’s house, injuring a mother. A big house near us is also ablaze, mortars and other weapons landed on it.“In front of our house I see a man injured and carried by hand. Civilians keep on fleeing the war, I see them walking with kids in hand and cursing the two warring groups.”At least two armoured vehicles were burned by opposition-allied militias, Mohamud Farah, another witness, said.Somalia has endured conflict and clan battles with no strong central government since the fall of the autocratic ruler Mohamed Siad Barre in 1991. The country has also been grappling with a nearly two decade-long insurgency led by the al-Qaida-linked al-Shabaab group.The US embassy in Mogadishu described the violence as “reckless”. “Somali leaders on all sides have a responsibility to preserve stability and resolve differences through peaceful means,” it said in a post on X.The UK also urged restraint and dialogue, saying the violence was unacceptable.
contentParagraphs.zh: 政府军和与反对派结盟的民兵在索马里首都摩加迪沙交火，造成财产损失，并迫使一些平民逃离。周四，由于总统哈桑·谢赫·马哈茂德决定在 5 月份任期届满后继续留任，暴力事件在周四计划举行的抗议活动之前爆发。今年3月，议会支持宪法修改，允许马哈茂德将任期延长一年并推迟选举。2009年至2012年执政的前总统谢里夫·谢赫·艾哈迈德表示，政府军袭击了他的家，并指责马哈茂德政府“非法修改宪法”。“政府军包围并袭击了我的房子。我从不害怕他们的侵略性攻击，我会反击，”他在自己的视频中说道。 Facebook账户连夜发布。 在X上的一篇帖子中，前总理哈桑·阿里·海尔指责政府军在人口稠密地区使用包括反坦克武器和无人机在内的重型武器。海尔补充说，政府指挥了“持续和不分青红皂白的军事袭击”，旨在杀死他和艾哈迈德。索马里的信息和国防部长没有接听电话，也没有回应路透社寻求置评的消息。居民告诉路透社，战斗于周三下午 5 点左右开始，一直持续到周四上午，数千名政府军部署在摩加迪沙的哈尔瓦达格和阿布迪亚西斯地区，他们在那里与支持反对派领导人的民兵交火。3 月，议会支持宪法这些变化可能会让索马里总统哈桑·谢赫·穆罕默德（如图）将任期延长一年。照片：Feisal Omar/Reuters 一位居民艾哈迈德·伊斯梅尔（Ahmed Ismail）周四早些时候告诉路透社：“一枚迫击炮弹落在我邻居的房子上，炸伤了一位母亲。我们附近的一座大房子也着火了，迫击炮和其他武器落在了上面。“在我们的房子前面，我看到一名受伤的男子被用手抬着。平民继续逃离战争，我看到他们手里拿着孩子，一边咒骂着两个交战团体。”另一位目击者穆罕默德·法拉赫说，至少有两辆装甲车被反对派结盟的民兵烧毁。自 1991 年独裁统治者穆罕默德·西亚德·巴雷倒台以来，索马里一直在没有强大中央政府的情况下经历了冲突和部族战争。该国还一直在努力应对由武装分子领导的长达近二十年的叛乱。与基地组织有联系的青年党组织。美国驻摩加迪沙大使馆在 X 上发帖称，此次暴力事件是“鲁莽的”，“索马里各方领导人都有责任通过和平手段维护稳定并解决分歧”。英国也敦促克制和对话，称暴力行为是不可接受的。

articles.title: Exams watchdog warns of rise in high-tech cheating
articles.summary: Ofqual chief says invigilators are being trained to detect devices like smart glasses and hidden earpieces.
articles.titleZh: 考试监管机构警告高科技作弊现象增多
articles.summaryZh: Ofqual 负责人表示，监考人员正在接受培训，以检测智能眼镜和隐藏式耳机等设备。

contentParagraphs.en: Exams watchdog warns of rise in high-tech cheating35 minutes agoJames W KellyTechnology reporterGetty ImagesThe growing use of smart technology could make cheating in exams harder to detect, the head of England's exams regulator has warnedSir Ian Bauckham, the chief regulator of Ofqual, said invigilators are being trained to spot covert equipment, including smart glasses, hidden earpieces and pens with built-in screens.Data from Ofqual shows that the use of mobile phones and smart devices has been the most common form of exam malpractice in every summer exam series since 2018. Last year, it accounted for 44% of all student malpractice cases.More than a million pupils are sitting GCSE and A-level exams this summer.Speaking on Radio 4's Today programme, Sir Ian said schools were reporting attempts to use increasingly sophisticated devices to gain an unfair advantage."We're hearing stories - and I hear this directly from schools as I go up and down the country - of devices like supposedly hidden earpieces, smart glasses that play text covertly on the inside of the glasses that only the wearer can see, and even biros that have got apparently invisible mini video screens built into them," he said.Ofqual figures from last summer include:2,225 cases involving mobile phones and smart devices545 cases resulting in students being disqualified from some or all of their qualifications1,240 cases leading to a loss of marks"In the worst cases, they could lose all their A-level grades. That's future-altering," Sir Ian said.Getty ImagesSmart glasses featuring cameras and AI is among the tech concerning Ofqual"Some of these devices are being marketed openly on the internet specifically as aids to cheating," Sir Ian said.He said the "vast majority" of students "wouldn't dream of cheating"."But there is this small minority - and it is a small minority - who have always set about trying to subvert the system and cheat," he added. Sir Ian said exam boards were providing training and guidance to invigilators and exams officers on how to identify suspicious devices and behaviour in exam halls.The Ofqual chief warned pupils that the probability of getting caught cheating is high and can lead to "really severe sanctions".More on this story
contentParagraphs.zh: 考试监管机构警告高科技作弊现象增加35分钟前詹姆斯·W·凯利科技记者盖蒂图片社英格兰考试监管机构负责人警告说，越来越多地使用智能技术可能会使考试作弊更难被发现。自 2018 年以来，这是每个夏季考试系列中最常见的考试舞弊形式。去年，它占所有学生舞弊案件的 44%。今年夏天，有超过 100 万学生参加 GCSE 和 A-level 考试。伊恩爵士在 Radio 4 的 Today 节目中表示，学校正在报告试图使用日益复杂的设备来获得不公平优势。隐藏式耳机、在眼镜内部秘密播放只有佩戴者才能看到的文本的智能眼镜，甚至内置了明显隐形的迷你视频屏幕的圆珠笔。去年夏天的合格数字包括：2,225起涉及手机和智能设备的案件，545起案件导致学生部分或全部资格被取消，1,240起案件导致分数下降“在最坏的情况下，他们可能会失去所有的A-level成绩。伊恩爵士说：“这些设备中的一些在互联网上公开销售，专门作为作弊的辅助工具。”伊恩爵士说。他说，“绝大多数”学生“不会梦想作弊”。“但有一小部分人——而且是一小部分人——总是试图颠覆系统并作弊，”他补充道。伊恩爵士表示，考试委员会正在向监考人员和考试官员提供如何识别考场中可疑设备和行为的培训和指导。Ofqual 负责人警告学生，作弊被发现的可能性很高，可能会导致“非常严厉的制裁”。更多关于这个故事

articles.title: President Trump seeks control of science funding
articles.summary: The White House Office of Management and Budget is moving to take more control of billions of dollars in federal grants. Critics say the proposed change would jeopardize the integrity of U.S. science.
articles.titleZh: 特朗普总统寻求控制科学经费
articles.summaryZh: 白宫管理和预算办公室正在采取行动，加强对数十亿美元联邦拨款的控制。批评者表示，拟议的改变将危及美国科学的完整性。

contentParagraphs.en: White House Office of Management and Budget Director Russell Vought appears before the House Budget Committee at the U.S. Capitol on April 15. The budget office recently proposed a rule change that would give political appointees more decision-making power over research grants.
contentParagraphs.zh: White House Office of Management and Budget Director Russell Vought appears before the House Budget Committee at the U.S. Capitol on April 15. The budget office recently proposed a rule change that would give political appointees more decision-making power over research grants.

contentParagraphs.en: The Trump administration is pursuing a bureaucratic rule change that could allow for greater political influence over billions of dollars in federal research grants. The new rule would have a broad impact on research fields, including housing and transportation. Health and science funding would be most significantly affected.
contentParagraphs.zh: The Trump administration is pursuing a bureaucratic rule change that could allow for greater political influence over billions of dollars in federal research grants. The new rule would have a broad impact on research fields, including housing and transportation. Health and science funding would be most significantly affected.

contentParagraphs.en: "Although research has bipartisan support in the US Congress, and trust in science is above 75% across the country, the Trump administration seems as determined as ever to mortally wound the nation's scientific enterprise," Holden Thorp, editor of Science magazine, wrote in an editorial about the proposal.
contentParagraphs.zh: "Although research has bipartisan support in the US Congress, and trust in science is above 75% across the country, the Trump administration seems as determined as ever to mortally wound the nation's scientific enterprise," Holden Thorp, editor of Science magazine, wrote in an editorial about the proposal.

contentParagraphs.en: Published in the Federal Register on May 29, experts say the proposed changes would both codify the administration's strategies to dismantle certain fields of study in the U.S. and lend it new authority to "advance the President's policy priorities."
contentParagraphs.zh: Published in the Federal Register on May 29, experts say the proposed changes would both codify the administration's strategies to dismantle certain fields of study in the U.S. and lend it new authority to "advance the President's policy priorities."

contentParagraphs.en: In science, the impacts could reverberate across fields of research as varied as public health, vaccine testing, biotechnology, social and behavioral science and climate science.
contentParagraphs.zh: In science, the impacts could reverberate across fields of research as varied as public health, vaccine testing, biotechnology, social and behavioral science and climate science.

contentParagraphs.en: The proposal is animating advocacy and science groups across the country.
contentParagraphs.zh: The proposal is animating advocacy and science groups across the country.

contentParagraphs.en: "This would be the end of American science as we know it," said Cole Donovan, a policy analyst from the group Stand up for Science who has been organizing to protest the change. "We're gonna make sure that it doesn't fade quietly into the night."
contentParagraphs.zh: "This would be the end of American science as we know it," said Cole Donovan, a policy analyst from the group Stand up for Science who has been organizing to protest the change. "We're gonna make sure that it doesn't fade quietly into the night."

contentParagraphs.en: End of peer review as the gold standard
contentParagraphs.zh: End of peer review as the gold standard

contentParagraphs.en: Since the post-World War II period, the U.S. scientific community has relied heavily on a system of peer review to offer feedback on studies and maintain integrity in research. The same has been true for federal science agencies when evaluating proposals for research funding. Typically, agencies adopt recommendations from independent advisory committees on issues including vaccine schedules, environmental standards, or census methodology.
contentParagraphs.zh: Since the post-World War II period, the U.S. scientific community has relied heavily on a system of peer review to offer feedback on studies and maintain integrity in research. The same has been true for federal science agencies when evaluating proposals for research funding. Typically, agencies adopt recommendations from independent advisory committees on issues including vaccine schedules, environmental standards, or census methodology.

contentParagraphs.en: While not legally binding, peer review in practice has been enormously influential and become part of the norms of government.
contentParagraphs.zh: While not legally binding, peer review in practice has been enormously influential and become part of the norms of government.

contentParagraphs.en: " While it's been true that peer review panels have always been treated as advisory by agencies, it was usually the combination of peer review with a non-political career expert at an agency that made the determination of whether to issue an award or not," said Donovan.
contentParagraphs.zh: " While it's been true that peer review panels have always been treated as advisory by agencies, it was usually the combination of peer review with a non-political career expert at an agency that made the determination of whether to issue an award or not," said Donovan.

contentParagraphs.en: Under the new rule, peer review would not be eliminated, but political appointees — not necessarily scientists — would be required to review grants before awards are made. Critics say that effectively gives political officials veto power over projects, even when they have passed scientific peer review.
contentParagraphs.zh: Under the new rule, peer review would not be eliminated, but political appointees — not necessarily scientists — would be required to review grants before awards are made. Critics say that effectively gives political officials veto power over projects, even when they have passed scientific peer review.

contentParagraphs.en: The reaction from scientists and advocates has been swift and vehement.
contentParagraphs.zh: The reaction from scientists and advocates has been swift and vehement.

contentParagraphs.en: " If this sort of rulemaking or rule-breaking becomes the norm, then government itself will cease to work," historian Tim Snyder said in an online forum Tuesday organized by Stand Up for Science. Snyder studies the former Soviet Union and remarked that the Trump administration's proposed rule change reminded him of "late Stalinism."
contentParagraphs.zh: " If this sort of rulemaking or rule-breaking becomes the norm, then government itself will cease to work," historian Tim Snyder said in an online forum Tuesday organized by Stand Up for Science. Snyder studies the former Soviet Union and remarked that the Trump administration's proposed rule change reminded him of "late Stalinism."

contentParagraphs.en: "We're asking ourselves whether we wanna repeat that Stalinist situation where people who know nothing about science are the ones who are making the decisions about what's going to go forward," said Snyder.
contentParagraphs.zh: "We're asking ourselves whether we wanna repeat that Stalinist situation where people who know nothing about science are the ones who are making the decisions about what's going to go forward," said Snyder.

contentParagraphs.en: Others compared the moment to a period in the United States in the 1950s when government officials scrutinized people's ideology and politics rather than their credentials.
contentParagraphs.zh: Others compared the moment to a period in the United States in the 1950s when government officials scrutinized people's ideology and politics rather than their credentials.

contentParagraphs.en: "Proposed rule would replace scientific merit with McCarthy era politics," read the headline from a statement by the Infectious Diseases Society of America.
contentParagraphs.zh: "Proposed rule would replace scientific merit with McCarthy era politics," read the headline from a statement by the Infectious Diseases Society of America.

contentParagraphs.en: The rule change was proposed by the White House's Office of Management and Budget, which is led by Director Russell Vought. Administration officials argue that the rule change is in the interest of efficiency. In a statement, an OMB spokesperson wrote it would "improve the ability of agencies to identify and respond to waste, fraud, and abuse."
contentParagraphs.zh: The rule change was proposed by the White House's Office of Management and Budget, which is led by Director Russell Vought. Administration officials argue that the rule change is in the interest of efficiency. In a statement, an OMB spokesperson wrote it would "improve the ability of agencies to identify and respond to waste, fraud, and abuse."

contentParagraphs.en: Critics say there's no evidence that the existing peer-review system needs such sweeping changes and that empowering political actors to make decisions about science is dangerous.
contentParagraphs.zh: Critics say there's no evidence that the existing peer-review system needs such sweeping changes and that empowering political actors to make decisions about science is dangerous.

contentParagraphs.en: "When we're designing a study to a new cancer therapeutic, do you want Russell Vought — who is not a scientist — to determine which immunotherapy is ready to go into a phase three trial?" said Elizabeth Ginexi, a former staffer at the National Institutes of Health who has been speaking out against the proposed changes.
contentParagraphs.zh: "When we're designing a study to a new cancer therapeutic, do you want Russell Vought — who is not a scientist — to determine which immunotherapy is ready to go into a phase three trial?" said Elizabeth Ginexi, a former staffer at the National Institutes of Health who has been speaking out against the proposed changes.

contentParagraphs.en: In addition to giving the administration increased authority, the proposed rule officially bans research on diversity, equity and inclusion or gender as grant conditions, and places broad prohibition on international scientific collaborations.
contentParagraphs.zh: In addition to giving the administration increased authority, the proposed rule officially bans research on diversity, equity and inclusion or gender as grant conditions, and places broad prohibition on international scientific collaborations.

contentParagraphs.en: " We are involved in a huge amount of international collaboration," said Donovan from Stand Up for Science. "Much of the work that's considered high-impact is based on international collaboration."
contentParagraphs.zh: " We are involved in a huge amount of international collaboration," said Donovan from Stand Up for Science. "Much of the work that's considered high-impact is based on international collaboration."

contentParagraphs.en: Limited role for congressional pushback
contentParagraphs.zh: Limited role for congressional pushback

contentParagraphs.en: Several Democratic lawmakers spoke at the meeting on Tuesday in an effort to rally support from the public.
contentParagraphs.zh: Several Democratic lawmakers spoke at the meeting on Tuesday in an effort to rally support from the public.

contentParagraphs.en: " When promising research is denied because it doesn't fit the political agenda of the moment, the American people will pay the price," said U.S. Rep. James Walkinshaw of Virginia.
contentParagraphs.zh: " When promising research is denied because it doesn't fit the political agenda of the moment, the American people will pay the price," said U.S. Rep. James Walkinshaw of Virginia.

contentParagraphs.en: " The question isn't whether politics will influence research under this proposal," the Democratic lawmaker added. "That's the point."
contentParagraphs.zh: " The question isn't whether politics will influence research under this proposal," the Democratic lawmaker added. "That's the point."

contentParagraphs.en: Beyond exhortations to action from the public, however, Congress is unlikely to take action on the rule change.
contentParagraphs.zh: Beyond exhortations to action from the public, however, Congress is unlikely to take action on the rule change.

contentParagraphs.en: The proposed rule is open for public comment until July 13. After that, OMB will review those comments before deciding whether to issue a final version.

contentParagraphs.zh: 美国宣布以色列和黎巴嫩之间达成停火框架，其中包括扩大黎巴嫩军队的控制以及停止真主党的袭击。半岛电视台的曼努埃尔·拉帕洛 (Manuel Rapalo) 解释了真主党拒绝谈判如何导致执行的不确定性。发布于 2026 年 6 月 4 日

articles.title: A handful of American households pay for AI. Is the future free — or a subscription?
articles.summary: Just 3% of U.S. households pay for AI for personal use. Sign ups are growing — even though Americans have subscription fatigue.
articles.titleZh: 少数美国家庭为人工智能付费。未来是免费的还是订阅的？
articles.summaryZh: 只有 3% 的美国家庭为个人使用的人工智能付费。尽管美国人已经厌倦了订阅，但注册人数仍在增长。

contentParagraphs.en: A woman uses a laptop as she lies on the grass in a park in the Manhattan borough of New York City on April 24, 2026.
contentParagraphs.zh: 2026 年 4 月 24 日，一名妇女躺在纽约市曼哈顿区一个公园的草地上使用笔记本电脑。

contentParagraphs.en: CHARLY TRIBALLEAU/AFP
contentParagraphs.zh: 查理·特里巴勒/法新社

contentParagraphs.en: CHARLY TRIBALLEAU/AFP
contentParagraphs.zh: 查理·特里巴勒/法新社

contentParagraphs.en: Stay up to date with our Up First newsletter, sent every weekday morning.
contentParagraphs.zh: 请随时关注我们每个工作日早上发送的 Up First 时事通讯。

contentParagraphs.en: Kirby Plessas doesn't have an AI subscription. She has two.
contentParagraphs.zh: Kirby Plessas 没有订阅 AI。她有两个。

contentParagraphs.en: As a self-described technophile, she uses chatbots outside of work to plan family parties, tweak cocktail recipes and once to diagnose a broken wine cooler's motherboard. All the help Plessas gets from AI justifies the $40 a month she pays for both OpenAI's ChatGPT and Google's Gemini.
contentParagraphs.zh: 作为一名自称为技术爱好者的人，她在工作之余使用聊天机器人来计划家庭聚会、调整鸡尾酒配方，并一度诊断出损坏的酒柜主板。 Plessas 从 AI 获得的所有帮助证明她每月为 OpenAI 的 ChatGPT 和 Google 的 Gemini 支付 40 美元是值得的。

contentParagraphs.en: "I wouldn't doubt that within a year I'll probably have a Claude subscription as well," she said.
contentParagraphs.zh: “我毫不怀疑，一年内我可能也会订阅克劳德，”她说。

contentParagraphs.en: But for most people, she believes, free AI is good enough. And judging by their spending, Americans agree.
contentParagraphs.zh: 但她认为，对于大多数人来说，免费的人工智能就足够了。从美国人的支出来看，他们也同意这一点。

contentParagraphs.en: Only about 3% of households were paying for AI in February, using the most recent numbers available from the Bank of America Institute, which researches consumer trends based on the bank's customer transactions.
contentParagraphs.zh: 根据美国银行研究所提供的最新数据，2 月份只有约 3% 的家庭为人工智能付费，该研究所根据银行的客户交易研究消费者趋势。

contentParagraphs.en: Yet while the number of personal subscribers remains small — plenty of workplaces and universities do pay for AI services — their ranks are growing fast. About 10% more households paid up in February compared to a year earlier, according to the institute.
contentParagraphs.zh: 然而，尽管个人订阅者的数量仍然很少——许多工作场所和大学确实为人工智能服务付费——但他们的数量正在快速增长。据该研究所称，2 月份支付的家庭费用比去年同期增加了约 10%。

contentParagraphs.en: "If you think back to Netflix and streaming services, at the beginning the growth was quite slow," said Sekoul Krastev, cofounder of the Decision Lab, a research firm with an emphasis on behavioral science. Krasteve said it isn't the norm to pay for AI — yet.
contentParagraphs.zh: “如果你回想一下 Netflix 和流媒体服务，一开始的增长相当缓慢，”专注于行为科学的研究公司 Decision Lab 的联合创始人 Sekoul Krastev 表示。 Krasteve 表示，目前为人工智能付费还不是常态。

contentParagraphs.en: "Once that status quo is created, subscriptions will definitely start to go up sharply just the way we saw with streaming services," he said.
contentParagraphs.zh: 他说：“一旦形成这种现状，订阅量肯定会开始急剧上升，就像我们在流媒体服务中看到的那样。”

contentParagraphs.en: Plessas puts it this way: "There's a thought out there that we're all going to get addicted to using AI. So when the free ones go away, everyone will have to pay."
contentParagraphs.zh: 普莱萨斯这样说道：“有一种想法认为，我们都会沉迷于使用人工智能。因此，当免费的人工智能消失时，每个人都将不得不付费。”

contentParagraphs.en: Subscriptions get you more
contentParagraphs.zh: 订阅让您获得更多

contentParagraphs.en: Even if they worry about how it will affect their daily lives, most Americans use AI. In fact, 51% of Americans said they use AI to research what they're curious about, according to a Quinnipiac poll released in March.
contentParagraphs.zh: 即使他们担心这会如何影响他们的日常生活，大多数美国人仍然使用人工智能。事实上，根据昆尼皮亚克大学 3 月份发布的一项民意调查，51% 的美国人表示他们使用人工智能来研究他们好奇的东西。

contentParagraphs.en: They're just using free versions. Most AI platforms offer them — to a point. For example, OpenAI lets users send its default model 10 messages every five hours before booting them to a weaker version.
contentParagraphs.zh: 他们只是使用免费版本。大多数人工智能平台都在一定程度上提供了这些功能。例如，OpenAI 允许用户每五个小时发送其默认模型 10 条消息，然后再将其启动到较弱的版本。

contentParagraphs.en: "You would get a little notification that says 'Your time has run out,'" said Pam Dean, who now subscribes to both ChatGPT and Claude. "You're stuck in the middle of something and then you couldn't continue."
contentParagraphs.zh: “你会收到一条小通知，上面写着‘你的时间已经用完了’”，现在订阅了 ChatGPT 和 Claude 的 Pam Dean 说道。 “你被困在某件事中，然后就无法继续下去了。”

contentParagraphs.en: The main selling point for subscribers is that they get to ask smarter models more questions, more often. A $20 monthly ChatGPT Plus subscription lets users send up to 1,280 messages a day before a downgrade.
contentParagraphs.zh: 订阅者的主要卖点是他们可以更频繁地向更智能的模型提出更多问题。每月 20 美元的 ChatGPT Plus 订阅可让用户在降级前每天发送最多 1,280 条消息。

contentParagraphs.en: Subscribers also get more advanced features, such as being able to create tailored versions of ChatGPT to fit their specific needs.
contentParagraphs.zh: 订阅者还可以获得更高级的功能，例如能够创建 ChatGPT 的定制版本以满足他们的特定需求。

contentParagraphs.en: To prepare for a possible move from Los Angeles to Mexico, Jim Arnold created "Francisco," a ChatGPT version that acts as his Spanish tutor. Francisco corrects Arnold's Spanish and has conversations with him through his computer speakers. Arnold fine-tuned Francisco to speak slower, and to take longer pauses before replying. That gives Arnold a chance to think of the right Spanish word before Francisco interrupts.
contentParagraphs.zh: 为了准备从洛杉矶搬到墨西哥，吉姆·阿诺德创建了“弗朗西斯科”，这是一个 ChatGPT 版本，充当他的西班牙语导师。弗朗西斯科纠正阿诺德的西班牙语，并通过电脑扬声器与他交谈。阿诺德对弗朗西斯科进行了微调，放慢了语速，并在回答之前停顿了更长的时间。这让阿诺德有机会在弗朗西斯科打断之前想出正确的西班牙语单词。

contentParagraphs.en: Still, Arnold never tried the free version of ChatGPT, and was wondering if paying was worthwhile. So he asked the AI. "And of course, you know, ChatGPT's answer was that I should keep what I'm doing for sure," Arnold said.
contentParagraphs.zh: 尽管如此，Arnold 从未尝试过 ChatGPT 的免费版本，并且想知道付费是否值得。于是他向人工智能询问。 “当然，你知道，ChatGPT 的回答是我应该保留我正在做的事情，”阿诺德说。

contentParagraphs.en: Today's version of ChatGPT will remain free …
contentParagraphs.zh: 今天的 ChatGPT 版本将保持免费......

contentParagraphs.en: OpenAI has about 50 million subscribers. Nick Turley, the head of ChatGPT at OpenAI, believes there's room to quadruple the number of users who pay.
contentParagraphs.zh: OpenAI 拥有约 5000 万订阅者。 OpenAI 的 ChatGPT 负责人尼克·特利 (Nick Turley) 认为，付费用户数量还有翻两番的空间。

contentParagraphs.en: Still, Turley said, subscribers will always be a minority of the people who use ChatGPT. He said the company's mission is to keep this tech accessible to benefit everyone: "And one of the ways that we do that is by making sure people can access it for free."
contentParagraphs.zh: 不过，Turley 表示，订阅者永远是使用 ChatGPT 的少数人。他表示，公司的使命是让这项技术惠及所有人：“我们实现这一目标的方法之一就是确保人们可以免费使用它。”

contentParagraphs.en: The birth of ChatGPT subscriptions wasn't about revenue, but keeping the site usable, Turley said. AI takes expensive computing power, and in the early days after ChatGPT's launch, putting up a subscription paywall helped the company keep up with demand while still allowing deeper access to those willing to pay up.
contentParagraphs.zh: Turley 表示，ChatGPT 订阅服务的诞生并不是为了收入，而是为了保持网站的可用性。人工智能需要昂贵的计算能力，在 ChatGPT 推出后的早期，建立订阅付费专区帮助该公司满足需求，同时仍然允许那些愿意付费的人更深入地访问。

contentParagraphs.en: "We weren't even trying to make money," Turley said about the launch of the subscriptions. But, he added, "It's been a really nice side effect that that also generates revenue and builds an amazing business."
contentParagraphs.zh: “我们甚至不想赚钱，”特利在谈到订阅服务的推出时说道。但是，他补充道，“这是一个非常好的副作用，它还能产生收入并建立令人惊叹的业务。”

contentParagraphs.en: …but you might have to pay for the next version
contentParagraphs.zh: …但你可能需要为下一个版本付费

contentParagraphs.en: Turley believes OpenAI will likely always offer a free version of ChatGPT. But he is not promising a free version of its eventual successor.
contentParagraphs.zh: Turley 相信 OpenAI 可能会一直提供免费版本的 ChatGPT。但他并没有承诺其最终继任者的免费版本。

contentParagraphs.en: That will likely be an agentic AI, a type of AI that can act on its own to do tasks for the user or an organization. Think of an AI agent that can plan a vacation for you, or do your shopping.
contentParagraphs.zh: 这很可能是一个代理人工智能，一种可以自行行动为用户或组织执行任务的人工智能。想象一个人工智能代理可以为你计划假期，或者为你购物。

contentParagraphs.en: OpenAI already offers agentic services, but Turley envisions a version that acts like a personal assistant, thinking of ways to improve your life while you're away from the screen. And that assistant could be so advanced and expensive to run that it might need to stay behind the paywall.
contentParagraphs.zh: OpenAI 已经提供了代理服务，但 Turley 设想了一个像个人助理一样的版本，在你离开屏幕时思考如何改善你的生活。而且该助手可能非常先进且运行成本昂贵，因此可能需要付费才能使用。

contentParagraphs.en: "If we do our job right, many people are going to want to pay and subscribe," Turley said.
contentParagraphs.zh: “如果我们做得好，很多人都会愿意付费订阅，”特利说。

contentParagraphs.en: Could AI companies use advertisements?
contentParagraphs.zh: 人工智能公司可以使用广告吗？

contentParagraphs.en: AI companies do have an alternative option to cover the cost of their services — advertisements. That's how many of the biggest websites, like Facebook, have kept their services free for years.
contentParagraphs.zh: 人工智能公司确实有另一种选择来支付其服务成本——广告。这就是 Facebook 等许多最大网站多年来一直免费提供服务的原因。

contentParagraphs.en: But Jeff Hancock, head of Stanford University's Tech and Impact Policy Center, said there's a reason AI companies should be cautious about adopting an ad model: "People hate it."
contentParagraphs.zh: 但斯坦福大学技术与影响力政策中心负责人杰夫·汉考克（ Jeff Hancock ）表示，人工智能公司对采用广告模式持谨慎态度是有原因的： “人们讨厌它。”

contentParagraphs.en: Ads incentivize social media companies to make sure users keep scrolling. The more screen time, the more ads get viewed. And endless scrolling has caused concerns about people's mental health and attention spans.
contentParagraphs.zh: 广告激励社交媒体公司确保用户不断滚动。屏幕时间越长，广告获得的浏览量就越多。无休止的滚动引起了对人们心理健康和注意力范围的担忧。

contentParagraphs.en: "People just don't like how social media fits into our lives now," Hancock said.
contentParagraphs.zh: “人们只是不喜欢社交媒体现在如何融入我们的生活，”汉考克说。

contentParagraphs.en: The second reason to be c
contentParagraphs.zh: 第二个原因是c

articles.title: 'I lost thousands in savings and my partner's money is in limbo'
articles.summary: The number of people withdrawing money from a LISA is outpacing the number using one to buy a home. Why?
articles.titleZh: “我损失了数千积蓄，我伴侣的钱也陷入了困境”
articles.summaryZh: 从 LISA 提款的人数超过了使用 LISA 买房的人数。为什么？

contentParagraphs.en: 'I lost thousands in savings and my partner's money is in limbo'6 hours agoMeghan Owen,London work and money correspondentandJonathan Fagg,England Data UnitBBCFraser Glen and his partner Sophie Bauer say the scheme has not worked for themThe Lifetime ISA (LISA) was set up in 2017 to help people save for retirement or purchase a first home costing up to £450,000. First-time buyers can put aside £4,000 a year and get an annual government bonus of 25%.But if savers are unable to find a property below the cap and need to withdraw the money, they face a financial penalty which costs them 6.25% of their savings.With the average first-time buyer now spending £463,000 in London, the scheme's property price cap is seen by many as being out of step with the capital's housing market.Based on figures from September 2025, BBC analysis found that the median LISA user could afford:The average flat in only 16 of London's 33 boroughs The average terraced home in only three boroughsThe average semi-detached home in one boroughNo borough had an average detached home within reachIn 13 boroughs, the median price for all types of properties was above £450,000. Those making unauthorised withdrawals outnumber the people using a LISA for a house purchase: in 2024-25, across the UK about 87,250 people made authorised withdrawals for a house purchase, while 129,200 made unauthorised withdrawals.We speak to young Londoners about their experiences with the scheme.'It hindered not helped us'Fraser Glen, 35, and his partner Sophie Bauer, 30, both started saving into Lifetime ISAs a few years ago with the intention of using the funds to get on to the property ladder.But after searching for flats in 2024 - looking at over 30 properties in central and east London - they realised how difficult it was to find one under the price cap that would meet their needs."People may think we're talking about luxury, big properties with big bedrooms, multiple properties - that's not what we were talking about at all," Fraser says. "We're talking about one, two-bedroom flats; the costs significantly more than £450,000 if you want to live within touching distance of central London where lots of us work."In order to buy their "modest two-bedroom flat" in Tower Hamlets for £521,000, Sophie withdrew her money from the LISA and lost £3,500. Fraser decided to keep his money in the LISA to avoid the penalty - leaving £50,000 of his savings "in limbo", as he can't access the money until he is 60 years old without losing a chunk of it."This is a savings tool which hindered rather than helped us, which leaves a bad taste," he says."What you're either doing is encouraging young people to move out of London where a lot of jobs and opportunities are, and then paying huge amounts to get in on the train, or you have to cash out like we did and take the loss," Sophie says.Calvin Kern wants to see the penalties for early withdrawal removedCalvin Kern, 23, has been saving in the Lifetime ISA for two years and hopes to buy a two-bedroom property with his girlfriend before he turns 30. Unable to buy in Stratford, they've now set their sights on Epping or Edgware, further east in Zones 4 and 5."It's more expensive than I thought. We've had to change what we're looking for. It's a bit frustrating. And if anything, the prices are going to go up." Calvin wants to see the penalty removed if somebody withdraws."If you don't have a safety net in London like a family... some people would be forced to take their money out and lose out on the 25%, which makes the situation even worse."London is so expensive and a lot of young people are forced to work here - having the pressure of the penalty in London and trying to make it... that can be stressful."Still, Calvin is trying to remain optimistic. "I think after all this time I am able to see some light at the end of the tunnel and make a plan, even if the plan is not 100% and it's not the life I imagined it would be."Jordan WaiteJordan Waite, 31, says he and his partner have made compromises to buy a flat with their Lifetime ISAsJordan Waite, 31, put most of his savings into a Lifetime ISA during the pandemic. In October 2025, he and his partner bought an ex-council flat in Archway for just under the £450,000 LISA cap.But he describes the search as a "massive struggle"."It's only when you start looking that you realise quite how little there is," he says.While buyers can find properties within the cap by compromising on location or taking on high service charges, he says it's difficult to find a "future-proof" home for those planning to start a family.Jordan and his partner were happy with the location but settled for a flat with an 82-year lease, which they now plan to spend £10,000 extending."It poses a bit of a problem when it hits 80 years in terms of remortgaging and whether we could sell it on."He says he would recommend the LISA to people living outside London, noting that a friend bought a house with one in Manchester. But in the capital, he describes it as a trap.Jordan wants the withdrawal penalty scrapped. "I would accept not getting the bonus, but the thing that really hurt was the penalty. It's not the golden scheme it was sold as."Helen Knapman, news and investigations editor at MoneySavingExpert, says the Lifetime ISA can help many savers get on the property ladder but needs reform.She points out that buyers forced to withdraw their savings because a property exceeds the £450,000 limit can lose part of their own money through the 25% withdrawal charge, and says she wants a "two-pronged approach" with both the penalty removed and the property price cap raised.Knapman says this is particularly important in London, where average first-time buyer prices are around £460,000, and argues the cap should rise in line with house prices.While the government is considering new support for first-time buyers, she says existing LISA users should not be forgotten.HMRC's 25% withdrawal charge on early or unauthorised L

articles.title: We finally know enough about how the brain breaks to focus on fixing it, experts say
articles.summary: The Allen Institute in Seattle says scientists have now learned enough about how the brain works to start fixing it when it breaks.
articles.titleZh: 专家表示，我们终于对大脑如何损坏有了足够的了解，可以专注于修复它
articles.summaryZh: 西雅图艾伦研究所表示，科学家们现在已经对大脑的工作原理有了足够的了解，可以在大脑崩溃时开始修复它。

contentParagraphs.en: Allen Institute scientist Aaron Garcia (right) and Executive Vice President Ed Lein (left) examine a section of human brain in the lab. The institute has launched the Brain Health accelerator, which will focus on using genetic therapy to develop treatments for a range of brain disorders.
contentParagraphs.zh: Allen Institute scientist Aaron Garcia (right) and Executive Vice President Ed Lein (left) examine a section of human brain in the lab. The institute has launched the Brain Health accelerator, which will focus on using genetic therapy to develop treatments for a range of brain disorders.

contentParagraphs.en: The $400 million initiative includes plans to develop new genetic therapies — a term that includes gene editing as well as traditional gene therapy — for diseases including Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, ALS, Lewy body dementia and Huntington's.

contentParagraphs.en: "The latest genetic treatments allow scientists to control the activity of particular genes," says Ed Lein, who directs the institute's brain health programs. "That opens up the possibility for very specific precision therapies for brain disorders."

contentParagraphs.en: The accelerator is an outgrowth of the BRAIN Initiative, an ambitious research program unveiled by President Barack Obama in 2013. The goal of this public-private partnership was to create tools that would allow scientists to see the brain's inner workings and, eventually, to develop treatments.

contentParagraphs.en: Jeff Carroll, for example, was a teenager when he learned that his mother had Huntington's disease, a fatal, inherited disorder that destroys brain cells.

contentParagraphs.en: "The whole reason I'm in science started with this frustration with not being able to understand what was happening with my mom," he says.

contentParagraphs.en: To Carroll, the solution seemed obvious: "Since we know that all the bad stuff in Huntington's comes from this one gene … let's get rid of that gene."

contentParagraphs.en: But finding a way to do that was beyond the reach of his own relatively small lab at a university. So Carroll joined the accelerator effort.

contentParagraphs.en: Its goal was to speed up science research. And it has done so by developing technologies that allow scientists to rapidly characterize and map millions of cells.

contentParagraphs.en: "We now have a complete description of the types of cells that make up the brain, and also the genetic underpinnings of their properties," Lein says. "This foundation then lets you study disease."

contentParagraphs.en: Allen scientists have already begun studying how Alzheimer's disease changes nerve cells in the brain.

contentParagraphs.en: "It affects very specific types of neurons that are lost early in the disease and then over the course of the disease," Lein says.

contentParagraphs.en: Genetic therapies designed to protect these neurons might delay or prevent symptoms, he says. Similar approaches might also work to preserve the neurons affected by Parkinson's or ALS.

articles.title: US House votes to end Trump’s Iran war: Does it matter?
articles.summary: Four Republicans join Democrats in rare rebuke of president. But Congress still far from able to stop further attacks.
articles.titleZh: 美国众议院投票决定结束特朗普的伊朗战争：这重要吗？
articles.summaryZh: 四名共和党人与民主党人一道罕见地谴责总统。但国会仍远远无法阻止进一步的袭击。

contentParagraphs.en: The United States House of Representatives has voted in favour of measures to halt President Donald Trump’s war on Iran as the conflict drags into a fourth month and both sides remain at loggerheads in peace negotiations.The vote on Wednesday marks the first successful effort by lawmakers to force the US to end a conflict that has had mounting catastrophic effects, from thousands of civilian deaths to global trade disruptions.Recommended Stories list of 4 itemslist 1 of 4US House passes Iran war powers resolution in rare pushback against Trumplist 2 of 4Netanyahu downplays US-Israel rift after Trump confirms criticismlist 3 of 4Does Israel have nukes? ‘Most of the world assesses they do,’ says Rubiolist 4 of 4Iran faces a new energy imbalance, but its options are limitedend of listIt also comes as opposition to the conflict has grown significantly within camps of Trump’s Republican Party due to the effects on Americans themselves and Trump’s failure to swiftly secure a concrete, lasting deal with Iran.But for now, the vote will remain largely symbolic because of Trump’s own presidential veto power on legislation and because of Republican dominance in the House and Senate – even though it marks a significant reprimand by lawmakers.Here’s what happened, why it matters and why it doesn’t mean that Trump can’t – or won’t – launch new attacks on Iran:Secretary of State Marco Rubio testifies before the Senate Committee on Appropriations on Capitol Hill, Wednesday, June 3, 2026, in Washington [Jose Luis Magana/AP]What happened?On Wednesday, lawmakers in the House, led by Democrats, voted to invoke the War Powers Act, which allows Congress to force an end to hostilities if the president does not get its authorisation after entering an armed conflict abroad.Since the start of the war, Democrats have argued that Congress, not the president, holds the right to declare war. They’ve repeatedly tried to force a stop to the US-Israel war on Iran based on that argument.However, the Trump administration has countered that the military operations in Iran do not require congressional approval.The War Powers Act, which has been in force since 1973, requires the president to seek lawmakers’ approval before entering armed conflict.Only imminent attacks on the US allow the president to unilaterally deploy troops. In such an instance, the president must inform Congress within 48 hours.If Congress fails to declare war afterwards, the president must withdraw troops within 60 days of entering the war.In the case of the war on Iran, critics argued that the US was not under any imminent threat: The US and Israel struck first.Trump also failed to withdraw thousands of US troops deployed to fight the war at its 60-day mark: around April 29.House Democrats, who hold a minority of seats in the House, have tried to invoke the act three times since the US and Israel ignited the war on February 28. However, all previous attempts had failed.How did the House vote?Wednesday’s vote count was 215 in favour of the resolution to restrain Trump and 208 against.The success for Democrats came after four Republicans switched sides in what appeared to be a public rebuke of Trump’s policies.While Republicans staunchly supported the war in public at its start, the mood has noticeably shifted as the US economy and global trade have been badly hit. Trump’s approval ratings have also dropped drastically.Republican lawmakers Tom Barrett of Michigan, Warren Davidson of Ohio and Thomas Massie of Kentucky broke party lines two weeks ago when the last vote was held. On Wednesday, Brian Fitzpatrick of Pennsylvania joined them.This residential building in Tehran was destroyed in US-Israeli air strikes on Iran [Abedin Taherkenareh/EPA]Does the House vote restrict Trump’s actions?Not necessarily. At this point, the yes vote is largely symbolic.The Senate needs to pass the resolution as well, but Republicans also hold a slim majority in the upper chamber.While Senate Democrats have been forcing votes to kick-start the process that would force a US halt to the war, Senate Republicans have so far mustered enough votes to reject the proposals.The latest vote to advance exit procedures was held two weeks ago with a 50-47 tally in the 100-member Senate. Four Republicans joined Democrats in voting in favour while Senator John Fetterman of Pennsylvania was the only Democrat to vote against the measure.While the results reflected growing disapproval from Republican senators, the count was not enough.Even if the Senate follows the House in invoking limits on Trump’s war on Iran, Trump could veto the resolution.In that instance, Congress would have to pass the measure by a two-thirds majority to override the president’s veto. That’s not impossible. However, it could be unrealistic in the current climate: Some Republicans are unhappy, but the majority still publicly support Trump.Is the US actually at war?Then there’s the question of whether the US is even at war right now and if the resolution applies at all.A ceasefire between the US and Iran has been in place since April 8, even if it’s fragile. The Trump administration argues that this means the US is technically not at war at the moment.On May 1, Trump declared the ceasefire meant a “termination” of hostilities, even though the US has continued a blockade of Iran’s ports and has hit Iranian ships. Tehran too has continued blocking the Strait of Hormuz.US Secretary of State Marco Rubio raised that argument when he faced lawmakers in a series of hearings on Tuesday and Wednesday regarding the war. The lawmakers invited him to submit more information about the US plans to exit the conflict in Iran as well as to detail plans for Venezuela, where the US abducted President Nicolas Maduro in January.In a sharp exchange with Senator Cory Booker, a Democrat, Rubio declared: “The [Iran] war is over.”However, Senator Jeanne Shaheen, a Democrat on the Senate Foreign Relations Committee, accused Rubio of lacking acco
contentParagraphs.zh: 美国众议院投票支持停止唐纳德·特朗普总统对伊朗战争的措施，因为冲突已进入第四个月，双方在和平谈判中仍相持不下。周三的投票标志着立法者首次成功地迫使美国结束这场冲突，这场冲突已造成数千名平民死亡和全球贸易中断等灾难性后果。推荐故事清单 4 项清单中的 1 项美国众议院通过了伊朗战争权力决议，这是对特朗普清单的罕见抵制2 of 4 特朗普确认批评后，内塔尼亚胡淡化了美以分歧 清单 3 of 4 以色列有核武器吗？鲁比奥主义者 4 表示，“世界上大多数人都这么认为。” 伊朗面临新的能源失衡，但其选择有限。此外，由于对美国人本身的影响以及特朗普未能迅速与伊朗达成具体、持久的协议，特朗普所在的共和党阵营内对冲突的反对情绪显着增长。但就目前而言，这次投票在很大程度上仍具有象征意义，因为特朗普自己对立法拥有总统否决权，而且共和党在众议院和众议院占据主导地位。参议院——尽管这标志着立法者的重大谴责。这就是发生的事情，为什么它很重要，为什么这并不意味着特朗普不能——或不会——对伊朗发动新的攻击：国务卿马可·卢比奥于华盛顿国会山参议院拨款委员会作证，2026年6月3日，星期三 [何塞·路易斯·马加纳/美联社]发生了什么？周三，以民主党为首的众议院议员投票通过了援引《战争权力法》，该法允许国会在总统在国外卷入武装冲突后未得到授权的情况下强制结束敌对行动。自战争爆发以来，民主党人一直认为，宣战的权利是国会，而不是总统。他们多次以此为由试图强行停止美以对伊朗的战争。然而，特朗普政府反驳称，对伊朗的军事行动不需要国会批准。自1973年起生效的《战争权力法》要求总统在进入武装冲突之前必须征求国会议员的批准。只有迫在眉睫的针对美国的袭击才允许总统单方面部署军队。在这种情况下，总统必须在48小时内通知国会。如果国会事后未能宣战，总统必须在参战后60天内撤军。就伊朗战争而言，批评者认为美国并没有受到任何迫在眉睫的威胁：美国和以色列先发制人。特朗普也未能在战争结束60天后即4月29日左右撤出部署的数千名美军。自2月28日美国和以色列挑起战争以来，美国曾三次试图援引该法案。然而，之前的所有尝试都失败了。众议院是如何投票的？周三的票数为215票赞成限制特朗普的决议，208票反对。民主党的成功是在四名共和党人在似乎公开谴责特朗普政策的过程中改变立场之后取得的。虽然共和党人一开始在公开场合坚决支持这场战争，但情绪已经明显转变美国经济和全球贸易受到严重打击。特朗普的支持率也大幅下降。两周前举行最后一次投票时，密歇根州的共和党议员汤姆·巴雷特(Tom Barrett)、俄亥俄州的沃伦·戴维森(Warren Davidson)和肯塔基州的托马斯·马西(Thomas Massie)打破了党派界限。周三，宾夕法尼亚州的布莱恩·菲茨帕特里克(Brian Fitzpatrick)加入了他们的行列。德黑兰的这座住宅楼在美以对伊朗的空袭中被摧毁[Abedin Taherkenareh/EPA]众议院投票会限制特朗普的行动吗？不一定。此时，赞成票在很大程度上是象征性的。参议院也需要通过这项决议，但共和党在参议院也占有微弱多数。虽然参议院民主党一直在迫使选票启动迫使美国停战的进程，但参议院共和党迄今已集结了足够的选票来否决这些提案。推进退出程序的最新投票于两周前举行，由 100 名议员组成的参议院以 50 比 47 的票数进行投票。四名共和党人与民主党人一起投票赞成，而宾夕法尼亚州参议员约翰·费特曼是唯一投票反对该措施的民主党人。虽然结果反映出共和党参议员越来越多的反对，但计数还不够。即使参议院跟随众议院援引限制特朗普对伊朗的战争，特朗普也可以否决该决议。在这种情况下，国会必须以三分之二多数通过该措施才能推翻总统的否决。这并非不可能。然而，在目前的气氛下，这可能是不现实的：一些共和党人不满意，但大多数人仍然公开支持特朗普。美国真的处于战争状态吗？接下来的问题是，美国现在是否处于战争状态，该决议是否适用。美国和伊朗之间的停火自4月8日以来一直有效，尽管它很脆弱。特朗普政府认为，这意味着美国目前在技术上并未处于战争状态。 5月1日，特朗普宣布停火意味着敌对行动“终止”，尽管美国继续封锁伊朗港口并袭击了伊朗船只。德黑兰也继续封锁霍尔木兹海峡。美国国务卿马可·卢比奥在周二和周三的一系列有关战争的听证会上面对立法者时提出了这一论点。议员们邀请他提交有关美国计​​划退出伊朗冲突的更多信息，并详细介绍针对委内瑞拉的计划。美国一月份在委内瑞拉绑架了总统尼古拉斯·马杜罗。卢比奥在与民主党参议员科里·布克的激烈交流中宣称：“（伊朗）战争已经结束。”然而，参议院外交关系委员会民主党参议员珍妮·沙欣指责卢比奥缺乏共识。

articles.title: Smartglasses and earpieces may worsen exam cheating in schools, says Ofqual
articles.summary:
Stronger checks likely to be needed in England to safeguard reputation of GCSE, AS and A-levels, says Ian Bauckham
Cheating in exams could be magnified by the new generation of wearable hi-tech devices such as smartglasses or invisible earpieces, according to England’s qualificat
articles.titleZh: Ofqual 表示，智能眼镜和耳机可能会加剧学校考试作弊现象
articles.summaryZh:
伊恩·鲍克汉姆 (Ian Bauckham) 表示，英格兰可能需要加强检查，以维护 GCSE、AS 和 A-level 的声誉
根据英格兰资格考试委员会的说法，新一代可穿戴高科技设备（例如智能眼镜或隐形耳机）可能会加剧考试作弊现象。

contentParagraphs.en: Cheating in exams could be magnified by the new generation of wearable hi-tech devices such as smartglasses or invisible earpieces, according to England’s qualifications watchdog.Ian Bauckham, the head of the Office of Qualifications and Examinations Regulation (Ofqual), also revealed that GCSEs and A-level courses in England were being scrutinised over potential AI use in students’ coursework, after teachers said they were struggling to detect it.Speaking on an Ofqual podcast, Bauckham warned that recent increases in cheating enabled by smartphones may be made worse by the next wave of wearable devices, undermining England’s school qualifications system.Bauckham said that the regulator had to act “really fast because technology is changing fast. We are all familiar with mobile phones but there are smartwatches that we are increasingly seeing on young people that are fully internet connected and so present many of the same challenges as mobile phones.“I understand that in the pipeline there are things like smartglasses that will play text across the inside of the lens that only students can see … so we are going to have to keep on top of this.“Our qualification system is a real national asset and we have to keep on top of this to stop this national asset being undermined, because that is not in anyone’s interests.”Ofqual said internet-enabled gadgets “including invisible earpieces and smartglasses” were already being advertised, while the number of students penalised for having mobile phones and other connected devices such as smartwatches in exam halls had continued to rise.Last summer Ofqual recorded 2,225 cases of mobile phone and smart device cheating associated with GCSE, AS and A-levels, which has been the largest category of exam cheating every year since 2018.“Obviously, if you gain help unfairly on a mobile phone or a smartwatch or any other kind of device, you are potentially getting marks in the exam that you don’t deserve,” Bauckham said.“The long-term consequences is that the grade you get at the end might not accurately describe the extent to which you have learned and mastered and demonstrated the content that’s being assessed. So you end up with grades for qualifications that are no longer reliable, no longer trustworthy.”Bauckham hinted that stronger checks were likely to be introduced to guard against students using AI in coursework submitted for A-levels and GCSEs, after being repeatedly told by teachers that AI-generated content was “getting harder and harder” for them to detect.“We’re looking very hard at that question now. GCSEs and A-levels are in the process of being refreshed or reformed, one of things we are asking … is, if there still to be coursework as part of this qualification, what do we need to put in place to make sure that the authenticity of that coursework can be guaranteed, in other words it really is the student’s own work,” he said.Aside from a “nuclear option” of dropping coursework altogether, Bauckham said teachers could be required to more frequently check with students about their work before signing off on it.“The other thing we can do is increase what we expect by way of referencing and sources, so that you actually explain where you have done your reading, where you have got the material that you are using. But what you haven’t done is just open ChatGPT and say: ‘Write me 10,000 words on Henry VIII’s foreign policy, please,’ because that is absolutely not acceptable.”
