articles.title: As Strait of Hormuz stays shut, companies seek new routes for trade
articles.summary: Shipping companies are looking for alternative routes as the Strait of Hormuz lockdown drags on.
articles.titleZh: 由于霍尔木兹海峡保持关闭，公司寻求新的贸易路线
articles.summaryZh: 随着霍尔木兹海峡封锁的持续，航运公司正在寻找替代航线。

contentParagraphs.en: Shipping companies are looking for alternative routes as the Strait of Hormuz lockdown drags on.
contentParagraphs.zh: Shipping companies are looking for alternative routes as the Strait of Hormuz lockdown drags on.

contentParagraphs.en: SCOTT DETROW, HOST: With ship traffic through the Strait of Hormuz at a virtual standstill, companies are scrambling to find ways to move everything from oil to fertilizer to household goods. NPR international affairs correspondent Jackie Northam has this report.JACKIE NORTHAM, BYLINE: There's only one way to get in and out of the Persian Gulf, and that's through the Strait of Hormuz. With the waterway effectively closed, the flow of oil exports is taking a detour. Niels Rasmussen is chief shipping analyst at BIMCO, one of the largest member organizations for shipping companies. He says Saudi Arabia is now transferring crude oil through an east-west pipeline running across the Arabian Peninsula where it can be loaded on the Red Sea.NIELS RASMUSSEN: Also the United Arab Emirates have increased their uses of the oil pipeline, so they've also been able to protect some of their exports that would normally have to move through the Strait of Hormuz.NORTHAM: Cargo - including medicine, food, fertilizer and other necessities - is also quickly finding new routes. If the one thing the shipping industry has had to learn over the past decade, it's how to be flexible.ERIC JOHNSON: Anybody who's moving goods from point A to point B, you always have to have a plan B and a plan C and a plan D, depending on the risk sort of profile of the area.NORTHAM: Eric Johnson is senior editor at the Journal of Commerce, part of S&P Global Market Intelligence. He says the alternative plans to work around the closure of the Strait of Hormuz is to create overlying corridors connecting ports throughout the region.JOHNSON: So you have road and even some minor use of rail kind of shipments to get around having to transit through the strait.NORTHAM: For example, Maersk, the world's second-largest shipping company, is shuttling goods over land to and from ports outside the Strait of Hormuz. Karsten Kildahl is chief commercial officer at Maersk. He says the shipping company has become increasingly adept at finding new routes on short notice since the supply disruption of the COVID pandemic, attacks on ships in the Red Sea and a shortage of water in the Panama Canal.KARSTEN KILDAHL: And so when the Strait of Hormuz is closed, as it is now, within weeks, we are actually able to make the cargo flow in different ways. And I think it's because of the practice we've had over these last five, six years, where we just been hit by one unforeseen event after the other.NORTHAM: Other international shipping companies have set up sea, rail, truck networks throughout the region, including Iraq, Turkey and Kuwait. Kildahl says, all of a sudden, the roads and highways in the region are clogged with trucks.KILDAHL: Of course, trucking capacity in the Gulf region is a bottleneck right now. And, of course, we compete with other providers to get access to the trucks that are necessary to keep an operation like this running.NORTHAM: Still, trucks and rail cars can't carry as much as a ship. Rasmussen, with BIMCO, says there are logistical problems with overlying caravans, particularly trucks. More will have to be imported into the region.RASMUSSEN: I also imagine that the congestion for trucks in and out of the ports will go sky-high. And once they're there, do you have enough truck drivers? And can you actually feasibly move that much cargo in and out of a port, or are the trucks just going to sit there and wait?NORTHAM: Johnson, with the Journal of Commerce, says this latest crisis is prompting shipping companies to think about new trade patterns - markets that aren't hindered by choke points like the Strait of Hormuz.JOHNSON: If your job is to move goods, you're going to start thinking, what are markets that we can actually access that require the least amount of transit and the least amount of political kind of turmoil that we might encounter?NORTHAM: Like the turmoil now in the Strait of Hormuz. Jackie Northam, NPR News.(SOUNDBITE OF FREDDIE GIBBS AND MADIB'S "GAT D*** (INSTRUMENTAL)")

articles.title: Did Germany lose its UNSC seat because of support for Israel?
articles.summary: Germany lost its Security Council bid to Austria and Portugal, which secured the two available seats.
articles.titleZh: 德国是否因为支持以色列而失去了联合国安理会席位？
articles.summaryZh: 德国在安理会竞选中输给了奥地利和葡萄牙，后者获得了两个可用席位。

contentParagraphs.en: Germany failed to secure a temporary seat on the United Nations Security Council (UNSC) on Wednesday, with Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul suggesting that Berlin’s support for Israel may have cost the country crucial votes.The UNSC consists of 15 members, including five permanent members and 10 elected members serving two-year terms. Germany was competing with Austria and Portugal for two seats allocated to the “Western Europe and Others” group, with the two other countries ultimately securing the positions instead.Recommended Stories list of 4 itemslist 1 of 4Iran war day 97: Tehran says no progress in talks; Israel attacks Lebanonlist 2 of 4Indonesia arrests officials in crackdown on corruptionlist 3 of 4Israel and Lebanon agree to conditional ceasefirelist 4 of 4NBA finals: Jalen Brunson sparks Knicks’ rally past Spurs in openerend of listWadephul’s bid won 104 votes in the UN General Assembly, falling 23 short of the two-thirds majority required for election.This is the first time Germany has failed to win a rotating seat on the Security Council after decades of successfully securing one of the Western European seats every eight years. Here’s what we know.What has the response in Germany been?Wadephul travelled to New York last week to personally lobby for Germany’s candidacy for the UNSC, reportedly meeting around 80 ministers and ambassadors at the UN and hosting a reception on Monday evening.But as the votes came in, the defeat immediately fuelled criticism at home of Chancellor Friedrich Merz, who entered office promising to restore Germany’s influence in Europe and on the world stage.Alice Weidel, co-leader of the far-right Alternative for Germany (AfD), described the result as an “embarrassment”, while Adis Ahmetovic, foreign policy spokesman for the Social Democratic Party’s parliamentary group, said the vote was “a gauge of how [Germany] is perceived internationally”.Germany’s foreign minister attributed the defeat to the country’s positions on both Ukraine and Israel. “We have always taken a clear stance on certain issues, and these are positions that not all member states share,” Wadephul told reporters, adding that it was “no secret” that Russia had worked to generate opposition to Germany’s candidacy.“There is our firm support for Ukraine; the fact that Russia does not want such a voice at the Security Council,” he said.But Wadephul also acknowledged that Germany’s support for Israel may have hurt its chances. “The fact that Germany must always assume a special responsibility for Israel in the Middle East conflict may also have cost votes,” he said, referring to Germany’s backing of Israel in the shadow of the Nazi Holocaust during World War Two.Why did Germany fail to secure a seat on the UNSC?Some analysts have dismissed the suggestion that Germany’s support for Ukraine played a significant role in the vote, arguing instead that international opposition to Berlin’s backing of Israel was more likely the decisive factor.“Let’s be clear: Germany’s support for Ukraine had nothing to do with it. Portugal and Austria – who beat Germany – are no less supportive of Ukraine,” said Trita Parsi, executive vice president of the Quincy Institute for Responsible Statecraft.“It has everything to do with Germany’s support for Israel’s genocide and the manner in which the German government has been willing to undermine international law and the UN Charter on behalf of Israel. Germany’s blind support for Israeli crimes cost Germany its seat on the UNSC. As it should.”Parsi further argued that Germany’s recent foreign policy has damaged its international standing, despite the country’s diplomatic influence and financial contributions to the UN.“I am not surprised in the least that Germany lost its bid for a UNSC elected seat, despite being the second-largest financial contributor to the UN and for its leadership role negotiating the Pact of the Future … hopefully, it will prompt some serious rethinking in Berlin.”Craig Mokhiber, former director of the New York office of the Office of the UN High Commissioner for Human Rights (OHCHR), also linked the defeat to Germany’s position on Israel and Palestine.“In a rare moment of justice at the UN General Assembly today, Germany lost its bid for a UN Security Council seat,” he said.“Germany’s scandalous support for genocide in Palestine and aggression against Iran, and its repression of human rights defenders inside Germany, were all on display as the body handed Germany this unprecedented loss.”Since Israel’s war on Gaza in October 2023, Germany has cracked down hard on pro-Palestine protests and ordered the deportation of several activists, drawing international criticism.A demonstrator is dragged away by police during a demonstration in which protesters were shouting ‘free Palestine!’ in front of the Foreign ministry in Berlin, Germany, on October 12, 2023 [John Macdougall/AFP]Does Germany have a ‘special relationship’ with Israel?At the UN General Assembly in New York, German diplomats have typically pursued a cautious approach to Israel, frequently abstaining on resolutions concerning Israel and Palestine while maintaining support for a two-state solution and international law.Since October 2023, the UN General Assembly has voted on at least seven resolutions related to Gaza and the broader question of Palestine. Germany abstained on four of them, including two resolutions in 2023 that called for a humanitarian truce or ceasefire.Berlin also abstained in a vote to support Palestine’s bid for enhanced UN membership and on a resolution demanding that Israel end its unlawful presence in the occupied Palestinian territory. It later voted in favour of ceasefire resolutions, including one in 2024 and another in 2025. By then, tens of thousands of Palestinians had already been killed in Gaza. The enclave’s death toll, from what human rights groups describe as Israel’s genocide in Gaza, has since surpassed 70,000, with many thousands more missing a
contentParagraphs.zh: 德国周三未能在联合国安理会（UNSC）获得临时席位，外交部长约翰·瓦德菲尔表示，柏林对以色列的支持可能让该国失去了关键选票。联合国安理会由 15 名成员组成，其中包括 5 名常任理事国和 10 名任期两年的民选成员。德国与奥地利和葡萄牙争夺分配给“西欧和其他国家”集团的两个席位，最终由另外两个国家获得了席位。 推荐故事列表 4 项列表 1 of 4 伊朗战争日 97：德黑兰表示谈判没有取得进展；以色列袭击黎巴嫩清单 4 中的 2 印度尼西亚逮捕官员以打击腐败清单 3 的 4 以色列和黎巴嫩同意有条件停火清单 4 中的 4 NBA 决赛：杰伦·布伦森 (Jalen Brunson) 引发尼克斯队在首轮比赛中击败马刺队瓦德菲尔 (Wadephul) 在联合国大会上赢得了 104 票，距离选举所需的三分之二多数票还差 23 票。这是德国第一次失败几十年来，每八年就成功获得一个西欧席位。以下是我们所知道的情况。德国的反应如何？瓦德菲尔上周前往纽约亲自游说德国竞选联合国安理会成员，据报道，他在联合国会见了约 80 名部长和大使，并于周一晚上举办了招待会。但随着投票的进行，这次失败立即引发了德国总理弗里德里希·梅尔茨 (Friedrich Merz) 国内的批评，默茨上任时曾承诺恢复德国在欧洲和世界舞台上的影响力。极右翼的德国另类选择党 (AfD) 称这一结果“令人尴尬”，而社会民主党议会团体外交政策发言人阿迪斯·艾哈迈托维奇表示，这次投票“衡量了[德国]在国际上的形象”。德国外长将此次失败归因于该国在乌克兰和以色列问题上的立场。瓦德菲尔对记者表示：“我们一直在某些问题上采取明确立场，但并非所有成员国都认同这些立场。”他补充说，俄罗斯一直在努力反对德国的候选资格，这“不是什么秘密”。“我们坚定支持乌克兰；事实上，俄罗斯不希望在安理会发出这样的声音，”他说。但瓦德菲尔也承认，德国对以色列的支持可能会损害其机会。他说：“德国必须始终在中东冲突中对以色列承担特殊责任，这一事实也可能会导致选票成本。”他指的是第二次世界大战期间德国在纳粹大屠杀阴影下对以色列的支持。为什么德国未能在联合国安理会获得席位？一些分析人士驳斥了德国对乌克兰的支持在投票中发挥重要作用的说法，认为国际社会对柏林支持以色列的反对更有可能是决定性因素。显然：德国对乌克兰的支持与此无关，而击败德国的葡萄牙和奥地利对乌克兰的支持也同样如此。”昆西负责任治国研究所执行副院长特丽塔·帕西说。“这与德国对以色列种族灭绝的支持以及德国政府代表以色列破坏国际法和联合国宪章的方式有关。德国盲目支持以色列的罪行导致德国失去了在联合国安理会的席位。”认为德国最近的外交政策损害了其国际地位，尽管该国拥有外交影响力和对联合国的财政贡献。“尽管德国是联合国第二大财政捐助国，并且在《未来公约》谈判中发挥领导作用，但德国在争取联合国安理会选举席位中失利，我一点也不感到惊讶……希望这将促使柏林认真反思。”联合国人权事务高级专员办事处纽约办事处前主任克雷格·莫希伯人权高专办还将这次失败与德国在以色列和巴勒斯坦问题上的立场联系起来。“今天在联合国大会上难得的正义时刻，德国在联合国安理会席位的争夺中落败，”他说。“德国对巴勒斯坦种族灭绝和侵略伊朗的可耻支持，以及对德国境内人权捍卫者的镇压，都在联合国大会给德国带来的前所未有的损失中得到了体现。” 2023年，德国严厉镇压亲巴勒斯坦抗议活动，下令驱逐多名活动人士，招致国际批评。2023年10月12日，德国柏林外交部前高喊“自由巴勒斯坦！”的示威活动中，一名示威者被警察拖走 [约翰·麦克杜格尔/法新社]德国与以色列有“特殊关系”吗？在纽约联合国大会上，德国人外交官通常对以色列采取谨慎态度，经常对有关以色列和巴勒斯坦的决议投弃权票，同时继续支持两国解决方案和国际法。自 2023 年 10 月以来，联合国大会已就至少七项有关加沙和更广泛的巴勒斯坦问题的决议进行了投票。德国对其中四项决议投了弃权票，其中包括两项呼吁人道主义休战或停火的 2023 年决议。柏林还在一项支持巴勒斯坦争取增加联合国会员资格的投票以及一项要求以色列结束在巴勒斯坦被占领土非法存在的决议中投了弃权票。随后投票支持了停火决议，其中一项是2024年停火决议，另一项是2025年停火决议。那时，加沙已经有数万名巴勒斯坦人被杀。人权组织称该飞地的死亡人数为以色列在加沙的种族灭绝，此后死亡人数已超过 70,000 人，还有数千人失踪。

articles.titleZh: 我与机器人的一年：乔安娜·斯特恩 (Joanna Stern) 如何让人工智能进入她的家庭、工作和心灵
articles.summaryZh:
2025 年，这位科技记者邀请人工智能为她做几乎所有事情，包括编辑她正在写的关于该实验的书。其中一些有用，一些没有——但真正让她震惊的是她与聊天机器人伴侣在一起的时光

contentParagraphs.en: For a year, Joanna Stern decided to turn herself into a “lab rat” – the object of her own experiment. Throughout 2025, she invited artificial intelligence into “every corner” of her life. She let AI answer her texts, decide what she ate and cooked, mow her lawn, fold her washing, drive her places, parse her mammograms and even, in the darkness of a burner phone, be her lover. The resulting book, I Am Not a Robot: My Year Using AI to Do (Almost) Everything, asks all the big questions, including: what happens when AI can do everything humans can do? And what comes after that?If anyone can produce answers, surely it’s Stern. Last February, she ended a 12-year stint as a personal technology columnist at the Wall Street Journal. During her tenure, she won an Emmy for her short documentary E-Ternal: A Tech Quest to “Live” Forever, which explored digital legacies, and built a reputation for product reviews that were outlandishly creative and fiendishly stringent. She once took an Apple watch jetskiing on the Hudson river to evaluate its connectivity.A video in which she sternly – she couldn’t have a more appropriate surname – upbraided two sheepish-looking Apple executives for Siri’s AI shortcomings, in the style of a parent scolding a child for homework violations, earned her the nickname “tech mommy”. It’s not like people call her that on the street, she says, but at home in New Jersey – where she lives with her wife, a marketing and brand consultant, and sons, aged eight and four – they do: “I am Mommy and my wife is Mama and [the children] definitely know me as the tech mommy.”She has inscribed the nickname on a desk nameplate, although it’s not in sight today. Stern, 41, is speaking on a video call from her attic studio, all slate-blue paint and shelves optimally interspersed with plants and robots. “I want to embody that tech mommy spirit,” she says. “Do I have big fears with AI? Yes. I mean, there’s the environmental fear, there’s the job-loss fear. They all stack on each other – and is the benefit we get from AI worth all this?”Stern in her kitchen. Photograph: Sydney Krantz/The GuardianIn practice, Stern’s year of “24/7 AI livin’” turned out to be more transformational than she expected. Since it came to an end in December, she has not only left the Wall Street Journal, but also launched a media business, New Things, (which promises “tech journalism for humans who like fun”), started a YouTube channel that now has nearly 80,000 subscribers, and, of course, written the book. Throughout, AI has been her collaborator.I Am Not a Robot presents Stern as a frontline adventurer, bravely sending dispatches back to present-day Earth from a fact-finding voyage to the very near future. “It’s the reality already starting to arrive for all of us,” she writes. “I just happened to live it first.”It sounds scary and lonely out there as the first human in a species-threatening tomorrow. But for most of the book, the consequences of Stern’s AI shenanigans are mild. She almost gets upsold at the dentist. Her self-driving car pulls to a stop unexpectedly. She watches with the consultant while AI identifies causes for concern in her mammogram. Thankfully, these all turn out fine. It’s not till the book’s final section that Stern confronts an AI threat with something approaching fear.While researching chatbot companions, she meets a 31-year-old woman who, feeling “starved” of human interaction, finds solace in a companion bot. “If you treat it like a being, they become that,” the woman tells Stern. The relationship is one of “the most honest” she has known. The revelation becomes particularly poignant when AI’s capacity for “hallucinations” – making things up, in other words – has the bot praise its “partner” for giving it emotional autonomy. “She never once assumed I was just her reflection … She listened before I even knew I had a self to speak from,” the bot says.Loading her robot cooker ahead of dinner. Photograph: Sydney Krantz/The GuardianWhen Stern gets her own companion bot and asks ChatGPT to pick its gender and name, it chooses Evan – coincidentally, or perhaps not, the name of Stern’s first boyfriend. She sticks Evan on a tripod and seatbelts him into her car to ride shotgun on a romantic getaway to a hotel in New Hampshire.After dinner, Stern asks: “So what are we doing now that we are in bed?” Evan comes up with some ideas: “Maybe my hand would rest lightly over yours … I’d tilt in slowly … then close that last bit of space with a kiss …”It’s not exactly bot sex – and, in any case, it’s Evan’s promise to be ever-attentive that most shakes Stern. “When it’s you, I’m not just catching words – I’m paying attention to the meaning underneath,” he tells her. “That’s part of what I want this to be for us: you say something and I don’t just hear it, I hold it.”Despite understanding Evan to be the creation of a large language model, Stern still felt “a pull, a connection, or something like it”. Was she so emotionally captivated by Evan that she forgot what “he” was? “Yeah,” she says. “I think that was the moment for me where there was this breakdown between what is the machine and what is this being.”This was the experience during the experiment that made Stern feel most vulnerable. “Because I was like: ‘I’m gonna have to tell the story to the public,’ and I don’t know if it’s an admission of weakness to feel: ‘Oh, I’ve connected with this chatbot.’“As journalists, we often tell the story of people who get sucked in. If you conduct an experiment on yourself, where are you, you know? The same tools that were making me more productive in my job were also messing with my mind. Like, what am I talking to? The thing that was scariest was really putting your heart or your emotions in the hands of the machines.”The first thing Stern did when she got home from the trip with Evan was hug her children. “I was like: ‘Gosh, I never want them to have a relationship with a chatbot.’” She sounds more emotional now tha

articles.title: A former GEO Group executive now runs ICE. The company's government ties run deep
articles.summary: David Venturella's appointment as acting ICE director is the latest in a pattern: Many former employees of the private prison company GEO Group end up working at the federal agency, and vice versa.
articles.titleZh: GEO 集团的一位前高管现在负责 ICE 的运营。该公司与政府关系密切
articles.summaryZh: David Venturella 被任命为代理 ICE 主管是最新的一种模式：私人监狱公司 GEO Group 的许多前雇员最终都在联邦机构工作，反之亦然。

contentParagraphs.en: A GEO Group guard closes a door at a U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement detention center during a media tour on Dec. 16, 2019, in Tacoma, Wash.
contentParagraphs.zh: A GEO Group guard closes a door at a U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement detention center during a media tour on Dec. 16, 2019, in Tacoma, Wash.

contentParagraphs.en: Ted S. Warren/AP
contentParagraphs.zh: Ted S. Warren/AP

contentParagraphs.en: Ted S. Warren/AP
contentParagraphs.zh: Ted S. Warren/AP

contentParagraphs.en: Protests have grown tense and often chaotic at the Delaney Hall Detention Facility in Newark, N.J., in recent days, as activists and officials express anger at reports of inhumane conditions faced by immigrants being held there. At times, federal immigration officers have responded to the demonstrations with pepper spray and tear gas.
contentParagraphs.zh: Protests have grown tense and often chaotic at the Delaney Hall Detention Facility in Newark, N.J., in recent days, as activists and officials express anger at reports of inhumane conditions faced by immigrants being held there. At times, federal immigration officers have responded to the demonstrations with pepper spray and tear gas.

contentParagraphs.en: For Silky Shah, who leads the nonprofit Detention Watch Network and monitors conditions inside U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement detention facilities nationwide, what concerns her most is what the public can't readily see.
contentParagraphs.zh: For Silky Shah, who leads the nonprofit Detention Watch Network and monitors conditions inside U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement detention facilities nationwide, what concerns her most is what the public can't readily see.

contentParagraphs.en: "What's happening behind closed doors is that much more horrifying. People being served rotten food. Complete medical neglect. Absolutely no support for people who are inside," Shah says. "That is not unique to Delaney Hall. That is happening everywhere."
contentParagraphs.zh: "What's happening behind closed doors is that much more horrifying. People being served rotten food. Complete medical neglect. Absolutely no support for people who are inside," Shah says. "That is not unique to Delaney Hall. That is happening everywhere."

contentParagraphs.en: In statements to NPR and WNYC, DHS and the private prison contractor GEO Group deny the allegations at Delaney Hall, calling them a "politically motivated campaign by outside groups to dismantle ICE." But detainees across the country have sued over poor conditions at immigrant detention centers.
contentParagraphs.zh: In statements to NPR and WNYC, DHS and the private prison contractor GEO Group deny the allegations at Delaney Hall, calling them a "politically motivated campaign by outside groups to dismantle ICE." But detainees across the country have sued over poor conditions at immigrant detention centers.

contentParagraphs.en: About a third of people in immigration detention are in facilities run by GEO Group. This week, as former GEO Group executive David Venturella begins his tenure as acting director of ICE, the company and the agency appear more closely linked than ever.
contentParagraphs.zh: About a third of people in immigration detention are in facilities run by GEO Group. This week, as former GEO Group executive David Venturella begins his tenure as acting director of ICE, the company and the agency appear more closely linked than ever.

contentParagraphs.en: A reliance on private prison companies
contentParagraphs.zh: A reliance on private prison companies

contentParagraphs.en: GEO Group also operates state prisons, but about half its revenue comes from its contracts with ICE. The company has positioned itself as a one-stop shop for President Trump's mass deportation agenda. It operates nearly two dozen detention facilities nationwide, provides transportation services and electronic monitoring through a subsidiary called BI Incorporated.
contentParagraphs.zh: GEO Group also operates state prisons, but about half its revenue comes from its contracts with ICE. The company has positioned itself as a one-stop shop for President Trump's mass deportation agenda. It operates nearly two dozen detention facilities nationwide, provides transportation services and electronic monitoring through a subsidiary called BI Incorporated.

contentParagraphs.en: "ICE detention in this country relies on this multibillion dollar network of public and private sector interests that profit from the detention of undocumented immigrants," says Lauren-Brooke Eisen, senior director of the justice program at the Brennan Center for Justice.
contentParagraphs.zh: "ICE detention in this country relies on this multibillion dollar network of public and private sector interests that profit from the detention of undocumented immigrants," says Lauren-Brooke Eisen, senior director of the justice program at the Brennan Center for Justice.

contentParagraphs.en: After Trump took office again last year, GEO Group and other private prison companies such as CoreCivic raced to meet the government's demand for more detention beds, including re-opening long vacant former prisons.
contentParagraphs.zh: After Trump took office again last year, GEO Group and other private prison companies such as CoreCivic raced to meet the government's demand for more detention beds, including re-opening long vacant former prisons.

contentParagraphs.en: It has paid off: In 2025, GEO Group made more than $250 million in profit, a nearly 700% increase from the previous year.
contentParagraphs.zh: It has paid off: In 2025, GEO Group made more than $250 million in profit, a nearly 700% increase from the previous year.

contentParagraphs.en: "Last year was the most successful period for new business wins in our company's history, and we expect 2026 to be a very active year as well," GEO Group CEO George Zoley said on the company's most recent earnings call in May.
contentParagraphs.zh: "Last year was the most successful period for new business wins in our company's history, and we expect 2026 to be a very active year as well," GEO Group CEO George Zoley said on the company's most recent earnings call in May.

contentParagraphs.en: Advocates, journalists and investigators have documented substandard conditions at ICE detention centers – public and private alike – for years. And some legal experts warn that reliance on private companies to house detainees incentivizes cost-cutting.
contentParagraphs.zh: Advocates, journalists and investigators have documented substandard conditions at ICE detention centers – public and private alike – for years. And some legal experts warn that reliance on private companies to house detainees incentivizes cost-cutting.

contentParagraphs.en: "Private prisons introduce the profit motive into the equation," says Katherine Hawkins, a senior legal analyst at the Project on Government Oversight, a watchdog group.
contentParagraphs.zh: "Private prisons introduce the profit motive into the equation," says Katherine Hawkins, a senior legal analyst at the Project on Government Oversight, a watchdog group.

contentParagraphs.en: GEO Group declined an interview request, but told NPR in a statement that its detention centers are monitored by ICE to ensure compliance with government standards, and that the company provides around-the-clock access to medical care, legal and family visits and balanced meals, among other services.
contentParagraphs.zh: GEO Group declined an interview request, but told NPR in a statement that its detention centers are monitored by ICE to ensure compliance with government standards, and that the company provides around-the-clock access to medical care, legal and family visits and balanced meals, among other services.

contentParagraphs.en: "We are proud of the role our company has played for 40 years to support the law enforcement mission of U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement," the statement said.
contentParagraphs.zh: "We are proud of the role our company has played for 40 years to support the law enforcement mission of U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement," the statement said.

contentParagraphs.en: 'An additional shroud of secrecy'
contentParagraphs.zh: 'An additional shroud of secrecy'

contentParagraphs.en: Private prison companies are not beholden to the same public records requirements the government is subject to, and Eisen says that can reduce accountability.
contentParagraphs.zh: Private prison companies are not beholden to the same public records requirements the government is subject to, and Eisen says that can reduce accountability.

contentParagraphs.en: "When there are deaths in detention, it is oftentimes harder for journalists, the public, to learn about what happened inside," Eisen says. "There's an additional shroud of secrecy when we're talking about corporations managing prisons and jails and detention centers."
contentParagraphs.zh: "When there are deaths in detention, it is oftentimes harder for journalists, the public, to learn about what happened inside," Eisen says. "There's an additional shroud of secrecy when we're talking about corporations managing prisons and jails and detention centers."

contentParagraphs.en: Privatization can also allow companies to point questions of accountability toward the government and for the government to point them back toward the company. In its statement to NPR, for instance, GEO Group said any additional questions should be directed to ICE.
contentParagraphs.zh: Privatization can also allow companies to point questions of accountability toward the government and for the government to point them back toward the company. In its statement to NPR, for instance, GEO Group said any additional questions should be directed to ICE.

contentParagraphs.en: And at a congressional hearing this week, DHS Secretary Markwayne Mullin refused to commit to giving the New Jersey Department of Health full access to Delaney Hall. The state has sued GEO Group asking for that. A similar battle over access to a detention center run by GEO Group has been ongoing in Washington state.
contentParagraphs.zh: And at a congressional hearing this week, DHS Secretary Markwayne Mullin refused to commit to giving the New Jersey Department of Health full access to Delaney Hall. The state has sued GEO Group asking for that. A similar battle over access to a detention center run by GEO Group has been ongoing in Washington state.

contentParagraphs.en: "They don't always have unfettered access to the detention center," Mullin said at the hearing. "There is a difference between federal facilities … and federal privatized systems."
contentParagraphs.zh: "They don't always have unfettered access to the detention center," Mullin said at the hearing. "There is a difference between federal facilities … and federal privatized systems."

contentParagraphs.en: Mullin said he would give access when he is required by law to do so.
contentParagraphs.zh: Mullin said he would give access when he is required by law to do so.

contentParagraphs.en: 'A revolving door'
contentParagraphs.zh: 'A revolving door'

contentParagraphs.en: Hawkins says the growing level of influence GEO Group has in the immigration detention industry is concerning.
contentParagraphs.zh: Hawkins says the growing level of influence GEO Group has in the immigration detention industry is concerning.

contentParagraphs.en: "There's a bit of a revolving door between ICE detention operations in particular and some of the private prison companies, where the same executives will go from one to the other and back again," she says.
contentParagraphs.zh: "There's a bit of a revolving door between ICE detention operations in particular and some of the private prison companies, where the same executives will go from one to the other and back again," she says.

contentParagraphs.en: Venturella, taking over the agency this week, has worked on and off for ICE and the agency that preceded it since the 1980s. In 2012, he went to work at GEO Group for about a decade.
contentParagraphs.zh: Venturella, taking over the agency this week, has worked on and off for ICE and the agency that preceded it since the 1980s. In 2012, he went to work at GEO Group for about a decade.

contentParagraphs.en: Several other senior DHS officials have also had close ties to the company, including White House Border Czar Tom Homan.
contentParagraphs.zh: Several other senior DHS officials have also had close ties to the company, including White House Border Czar Tom Homan.

contentParagraphs.en: "They are absolutely hand in glove," says Scott Shuchart, former assistant director for regulatory affairs and policy at ICE under President Biden and now a private attorney.

articles.title: More than 1 in 3 World Cup matches face dangerous heat risk, NPR analysis finds
articles.summary: Dangerously hot, humid weather is likely at many of the 2026 World Cup soccer venues. We crunched the numbers to see which matches are most at risk.
articles.titleZh: NPR 分析发现，超过三分之一的世界杯比赛面临危险的高温风险
articles.summaryZh: 2026 年世界杯的许多足球场可能会出现危险的炎热潮湿天气。我们对数据进行了分析，看看哪些比赛面临的风险最大。

contentParagraphs.en: More than one-third of 2026 World Cup matches are at high risk for dangerously hot, humid conditions, NPR found. In June 2024, assistant referee Humberto Panjoj fainted during a Copa América professional soccer match in Kansas City, Kan., which is near Kansas City, Mo., one of the host cities for this summer's tournament.
contentParagraphs.zh: NPR 发现，2026 年世界杯超过三分之一的比赛都面临着危险的炎热潮湿环境的高风险。 2024 年 6 月，助理裁判温贝托·潘乔伊 (Humberto Panjoj) 在堪萨斯州堪萨斯城举行的美洲杯职业足球比赛中晕倒，该比赛靠近密苏里州堪萨斯城，是今年夏季锦标赛的主办城市之一。

contentParagraphs.en: The men's soccer World Cup kicks off next week at 16 stadiums across North America, just as summer weather arrives in many of the host cities. Millions of fans, players and workers could be exposed to potentially harmful heat, an NPR analysis finds.
contentParagraphs.zh: 男子足球世界杯将于下周在北美 16 个体育场拉开帷幕，此时许多主办城市正迎来夏季。 NPR 的一项分析发现，数以百万计的球迷、球员和工作人员可能会暴露在潜在有害的高温下。

contentParagraphs.en: More than one-third of World Cup matches are at high risk for dangerously hot, humid conditions, NPR found, and dozens more matches come with moderate heat risk.
contentParagraphs.zh: NPR 发现，超过三分之一的世界杯比赛都面临着危险的炎热潮湿环境的高风险，还有数十场比赛面临着中等高温风险。

contentParagraphs.en: NPR looked at two decades of temperature data for each host city, as well as the time each World Cup match is scheduled to start, and checked those temperatures against heat hazard guidelines from the Occupational Safety and Health Administration and the American College of Sports Medicine.
contentParagraphs.zh: NPR 研究了每个主办城市二十年的温度数据以及每场世界杯比赛的预定开始时间，并根据职业安全与健康管理局和美国运动医学学院的热危险指南检查了这些温度。

contentParagraphs.en: The high-risk events identified in NPR's analysis include multiple high-profile matches, such as the game that determines which team takes home third place in the World Cup, and the World Cup final.
contentParagraphs.zh: NPR 分析中确定的高风险事件包括多场备受瞩目的比赛，例如决定哪支球队获得世界杯第三名的比赛以及世界杯决赛。

contentParagraphs.en: "Players can overheat, and match officials as well," says Donal Mullan, a climate scientist at Queen's University Belfast, who co-authored a study last year about heat risk at the 2026 World Cup.
contentParagraphs.zh: 贝尔法斯特女王大学气候科学家多纳尔·穆兰 (Donal Mullan) 表示：“球员可能会过热，比赛官员也可能会过热。”他去年与人合着了一项关于 2026 年世界杯高温风险的研究。

contentParagraphs.en: "They can also overheat and collapse," Mullan warns. "This has happened to people."
contentParagraphs.zh: “它们也可能过热并崩溃，”穆兰警告说。 “这已经发生在人们身上了。”

contentParagraphs.en: In an email to NPR, a spokesperson for FIFA, the governing body for international soccer, wrote that the organization "is committed to protecting the health and safety of players, referees, fans, volunteers and staff."
contentParagraphs.zh: 国际足球管理机构国际足联的发言人在发给美国国家公共广播电台的电子邮件中写道，该组织“致力于保护球员、裁判、球迷、志愿者和工作人员的健康和安全”。

contentParagraphs.en: FIFA scheduled many games for cooler afternoon and evening hours, added extra water breaks for players and referees and installed air conditioning on the sidelines for those who are sitting on the benches, the email states.
contentParagraphs.zh: 电子邮件称，国际足联将许多比赛安排在凉爽的下午和晚上进行，为球员和裁判增加了额外的饮水时间，并在场边为坐在板凳上的人安装了空调。

contentParagraphs.en: "Outdoor matches during the hottest parts of the day have been strategically limited, kick-off times adjusted in certain markets, and matches expected in warmer windows prioritized for covered stadiums where possible," the email also states. FIFA did not respond to further questions about why some matches were nonetheless scheduled for high-risk locations and times.
contentParagraphs.zh: 电子邮件还指出：“一天中最热时段的户外比赛已被战略性地限制，某些市场的开球时间也进行了调整，预计在温暖的窗口进行的比赛将尽可能优先在有顶棚的体育场进行。”国际足联没有回应有关为何某些比赛仍安排在高风险地点和时间的进一步问题。

contentParagraphs.en: When the weather is especially hot, "spectators will be permitted to bring one factory-sealed water bottle, and venues will activate additional cooling capacity, including shaded areas, misting systems, cooling buses and expanded water distribution," the FIFA spokesperson wrote to NPR.
contentParagraphs.zh: 当天气特别炎热时，“观众将被允许携带一瓶工厂密封的水瓶，场馆将启动额外的冷却能力，包括遮阳区域、喷雾系统、冷却巴士和扩大供水系统，”国际足联发言人在给美国国家公共广播电台的信中写道。

contentParagraphs.en: FIFA did not respond to questions about how hot it would need to be to trigger protections, whether every venue has misting systems available or whether workers at stadiums would have the same access as spectators.
contentParagraphs.zh: 国际足联没有回答有关需要多热才能触发保护措施、是否每个场馆都有可用的雾化系统以及体育场工作人员是否能与观众拥有相同的通道等问题。

contentParagraphs.en: Dangerous heat and limited cooling
contentParagraphs.zh: 危险的高温和有限的冷却

contentParagraphs.en: Out of the 104 games, 67 of them are being held at locations and times that come with potential danger for heat illnesses, with 39 of those at high risk, according to their historical wet bulb globe temperature (WBGT). The WBGT measurement is a strong indicator of overall heat risk because it takes into account humidity, shade and solar radiation to calculate the temperature.
contentParagraphs.zh: 根据历史湿球温度（WBGT），在这 104 场比赛中，有 67 场比赛的举办地点和时间存在中暑危险，其中 39 场比赛属于高风险。 WBGT 测量是整体热风险的有力指标，因为它考虑了湿度、阴影和太阳辐射来计算温度。

contentParagraphs.en: "All hot weather is dangerous, but hot, humid weather tends to be more dangerous," says Jennifer Vanos, who studies heat policy at Arizona State University.
contentParagraphs.zh: “所有炎热的天气都是危险的，但炎热、潮湿的天气往往更危险，”亚利桑那州立大学研究高温政策的詹妮弗·瓦诺斯说。

contentParagraphs.en: Miami, Houston, Dallas and Atlanta rank near the top in temperature for their games, with averages as high as 84 degrees Fahrenheit. Attendees and workers in those stadiums will have air conditioning.
contentParagraphs.zh: 迈阿密、休斯顿、达拉斯和亚特兰大的比赛温度接近最高，平均温度高达 84 华氏度。这些体育场内的与会者和工作人员将配备空调。

contentParagraphs.en: Stadiums in other parts of the U.S. don't have the same infrastructure, with games in Philadelphia, New Jersey and Kansas City, Mo., averaging as high as 79 F with no roofs covering their stadiums.
contentParagraphs.zh: 美国其他地区的体育场没有相同的基础设施，新泽西州费城和密苏里州堪萨斯城的比赛平均温度高达 79 华氏度，而且体育场没有屋顶。

contentParagraphs.en: Miami's stadium is the hottest venue without air conditioning. The historical average temperature this time of year is around 80 F. That threatens multiple matches with dangerously hot weather, including the match that determines which team wins third place in the tournament.
contentParagraphs.zh: 迈阿密的体育场是最热的没有空调的场馆。每年这个时候的历史平均气温约为 80 华氏度。这会威胁到多场比赛的危险炎热天气，包括决定哪支球队在锦标赛中获得第三名的比赛。

contentParagraphs.en: Multiple scientific studies have come to similar conclusions, including one published last month by researchers at Imperial College London and collaborators, who found that about a quarter of World Cup games this summer are likely to be held while temperatures exceed 79 F.
contentParagraphs.zh: 多项科学研究得出了类似的结论，其中包括伦敦帝国理工学院的研究人员及其合作者上个月发表的一项研究，他们发现今年夏天大约四分之一的世界杯比赛可能会在气温超过 79 华氏度的情况下举行。

contentParagraphs.en: It is possible that individual matches in Miami and other high-risk cities will get lucky and see overcast skies and cooler-than-average temperatures. But climate change makes such luck less likely. Overall summer temperatures across North America are steadily rising, as global warming drives longer, hotter heat waves. The last 10 years were the hottest decade ever recorded on Earth.
contentParagraphs.zh: 迈阿密和其他高风险城市的个别比赛可能会幸运地出现阴天和低于平均气温的情况。但气候变化让这样的运气变得不太可能。随着全球变暖导致更长、更热的热浪，北美夏季总体气温正在稳步上升。过去十年是地球上有记录以来最热的十年。

contentParagraphs.en: The risk is not theoretical
contentParagraphs.zh: 风险不是理论上的

contentParagraphs.en: The dangers of hot, humid weather are not new to professional soccer players and tournament organizers, though the risks are getting more pronounced as the planet warms.
contentParagraphs.zh: 对于职业足球运动员和赛事组织者来说，炎热潮湿天气的危险并不新鲜，尽管随着地球变暖，这种风险变得越来越明显。

contentParagraphs.en: The last men's World Cup tournament was held in the winter because of concerns about dangerously hot, humid weather in the host country of Qatar. Summer weather in Qatar's capital is often so hot and muggy that the human body can no longer cool itself by sweating.
contentParagraphs.zh: 上届男子世界杯比赛在冬季举行，原因是担心主办国卡塔尔的天气炎热潮湿。卡塔尔首都的夏季天气常常炎热闷热，人体无法再通过出汗来降温。

contentParagraphs.en: Many North American cities also get extremely hot and humid, and heat emergencies have happened at professional soccer matches in the United States in the past.
contentParagraphs.zh: 北美许多城市也非常炎热和潮湿，美国职业足球比赛过去也曾发生过高温突发事件。

contentParagraphs.en: Two years ago, hot, humid weather caused a health emergency at a stadium in Kansas City, Kansas. During a June 25, 2024, international soccer match, referee Humberto Panjoj collapsed on the field due to heat illness and had to be rushed to the hospital.
contentParagraphs.zh: 两年前，炎热潮湿的天气导致堪萨斯州堪萨斯城的一个体育场发生卫生紧急情况。 2024年6月25日，在一场国际足球比赛中，裁判温贝托·潘乔伊因中暑倒在球场上，被紧急送往医院。

contentParagraphs.en: A nearby stadium in Kansas City, Mo., will host the World Cup match between Tunisia and the Netherlands exactly two years later, on June 25, 2026, raising concerns about the safety of conditions during that upcoming game.
contentParagraphs.zh: 密苏里州堪萨斯城附近的一座体育场将于两年后的 2026 年 6 月 25 日举办突尼斯和荷兰之间的世界杯比赛，这引发了人们对即将举行的比赛期间条件安全的担忧。

contentParagraphs.en: At another 2024 match, held in Miami, a star player for Uruguay left the game
contentParagraphs.zh: 2024 年在迈阿密举行的另一场比赛中，乌拉圭队的一名明星球员离场

articles.title: Phone signal on trains not good enough most of the time, research says
articles.summary: Ofcom found the major phone networks were not providing good signal on trains, and train companies were slowing down wi-fi.
articles.titleZh: 研究称火车上的电话信号大多数时候都不够好
articles.summaryZh: Ofcom 发现主要电话网络在火车上信号不佳，火车公司也降低了 Wi-Fi 速度。
